EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated thunderstorm winds gusts of 50 to 60 MPH. They also reported that heavy downpours occurred with the thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops, powerlines, and structures along its path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds snapped numerous power poles, and downed a 3 foot diameter tree as well as many 2 to 4 inch diameter branches. Power was knocked out for several hours when falling tree limbs took down powerlines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th. With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward. Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant portion of the roof of a livestock building was removed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early morning storms associated with a weakening low resulted in one stronger cell in central Iowa producing quarter sized hail before dawn. Later in the day, a deepening low across the central plains lifted into Iowa with storms developing along a dry line in the evening. These storms produced hail up to baseball sized, along with a brief tornado remained rural and did not cause any damage. Additional convection was reinvigorated overnight thanks to the low level et, but remained sub severe. Areas that received multiple rounds of storms saw two to three inches of much needed rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 54 knot gust recorded on ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong system developed on the morning of March 31st and tracked across the state of Iowa. This system pulled unseasonably warm and moist air into the Midwest, which combined with a very favorable wind profile to create a volatile environment for severe thunderstorms.  Discrete supercell thunderstorms occurred initially, but quickly transitioned into a QLCS mode. This system produced a historic tornado outbreak for the area, with a record 29 tornadoes on the day, including 13 rated EF-2, 1 rated EF-3, and 1 rated EF-4. This was the first EF-4 tornado in the NWS Quad Cities service area since 1995. 11 injuries were reported, with no known fatalities. In addition to the numerous tornadoes, there were many large hail and damaging wind reports. Maximum straight line thunderstorm winds were estimated at 80 to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a semi tipped over as a line of thunderstorms moved into the area. The spotter measured winds in excess of 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms initiated west of the area over portions of Nebraska and Kansas in the overnight hours of the 28th into the 29th, eventually leading into and across southern Iowa during the morning hours of the 29th. These storms were situated within a fairly supportive environment, including a north south gradient of MUCAPE values in the 2000 to 4000+ J/kg range, 30 to 40+ kts of effective shear, and steering flow supportive of MCS maintenance. Eventually, linear storm mode evolved into an MCV. Resulting storms produced a number of strong wind and wind damage reports, including trees downed and measured winds to 67 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported power lines and trees down. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In semi similar fashion to the strong and severe storms experienced on the previous morning (29th of June), storms initiated over portions of northern Kansas and southern Nebraska in conjunction with the low level jet in the very early hours of the 29th. These storms, steered by easterly mean flow, progressed across far southern Iowa and northern Missouri within an environment consisting of around 2000 J/kg MUCAPE, 30 to 40+ kts of effective shear, and a very tight north-south gradient of DCAPE (most favorably into Missouri). Storms grew upscale through the early morning hours and primarily produced strong and damaging winds. There were a couple of severe hail reports as well along with a reported lightning strike that effective blew a tree apart.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported tree branch down on powerlines. Time estimated from radar.

An arc of convection developed ahead of a slow-moving upper-level low across central Nebraska. With around 2000 J/kg MUCAPE in a narrow zone and around 40 knots of deep layer shear, a few rotating severe storms developed with enhanced wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed numerous 1 to 2 inch diameter tree branches, as well as one limb up to 4 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of severe thunderstorms impacted the area on Sunday May 7, 2023.  Two areas of storms were present during the mid to late afternoon, one entering the Cedar Rapids and Iowa City metros and another around the Burlington and Fort Madison metros. The first was the most severe, producing hail up to baseball size in Koszta, Iowa and widespread wind damage, especially around West Liberty, Iowa. This storm, and storms to the west, eventually merged into a cluster of severe thunderstorms, producing large hail and destructive winds around 70-75 mph all the way to Mount Pleasant, Iowa and Macomb, Illinois. The latter produced sporadic reports of quarter to half dollar sized hail, along with a brief landspout tornado east of Yorktown, IL.  There was also severe blowing dust which was especially bad near I-55 in north central Illinois. Even more thunderstorms developed overnight across the area, and mainly produced torrential rain and isolated large hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated 70 mph winds near G Avenue and 212.

Two areas of storms were present during the mid to late afternoon, one entering the Cedar Rapids and Iowa City metros and another around the Burlington and Fort Madison metros. The first was the most severe, producing hail up to baseball size in Koszta, Iowa and widespread wind damage, especially around West Liberty, Iowa. This storm, and storms to the west, eventually merged into a cluster of severe thunderstorms, producing large hail and destructive winds around 70-75 mph all the way to Mount Pleasant, Iowa and Macomb, Illinois. The latter produced sporadic reports of quarter to half dollar sized hail, along with a brief landspout tornado east of Yorktown, IL.  There was also severe blowing dust which was especially bad near I-55 in north central Illinois. Even more thunderstorms developed overnight across the area, and mainly produced torrential rain and isolated large hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gutters blown off, with a 10 inch diameter branch down. A dumpster also blew down an alley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper air analysis depicted a negatively tilted trough over the western CONUS, with its axis extending from the central Rocky Mountains northwest into the Pacific Northwest. At the surface, a low-pressure center was deepening over the northern High Plains along with a secondary surface low in western Nebraska. Extending south out of these pressure centers was a Pacific cold front. At 21 UTC, a warm front was noted to extend southeast from southwest Nebraska into northeast Oklahoma. As the evening progressed, strong warm air advection ahead of the mature low-pressure pushed the warm front north into eastern Nebraska by 00 UTC. ||Along this warm front and ahead of the Pacific cold front, several lines of strong to severe thunderstorms moved through eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. The first line of strong storms moved through central and northeast Nebraska after 4:30 PM CDT. No severe reports were associated with these storms in the OAX CWA. Another intensifying segment of the same line moved into southeast Nebraska by 7:30 PM CDT, this time south of the northward advancing warm front. These storms tracked northeast through southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa, producing numerous severe wind gusts across the area and multiple spin-up tornadoes. The vast majority of wind gusts recorded with these storms ranged from 55 to 70 miles per hour. The strongest wind gust was measured at 71 miles per hour in Richardson County. 2 EF-0 tornadoes were confirmed in southeast Nebraska, both in Gage County. There were also an additional 3 tornadoes confirmed in southwest Iowa, all EF-1 tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 67 miles per hour wind gust was measured by a personal weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across a good portion of eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois on the afternoon of Tuesday August 24th, 2021. A corridor of very strong winds was observed along the Highway 20 corridor from Fairbank IA, to Hazelton IA and to Manchester IA, where numerous reports of damage to trees, homes and power outages were received. Hail up to golf ball size was also observed in Manchester IA.||Storms continued to track south and east, with an outflow boundary producing strong winds into portions of northwest Illinois including the Quad Cities metro. Additional reports of wind damage were received across northwest Illinois, with a 66 MPH wind gust measured at the Savanna, IL AWOS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were uprooted south of Hazelton, near the Fontana turnoff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of storms moved across northeast Iowa during the late afternoon and evening of August 27th. These storms produced two tornadoes in the vicinity of Marble Rock (Floyd County). The first tornado was an EF0 west of Marble Rock that only caused some minor crop and structural damage to a farm. The second tornado was rated as an EF1 that passed just to the south of Marble Rock as it moved southeast. Nearly all the buildings on one farm were destroyed and several other farm buildings and homes sustained some damage. The storms also produced heavy rain with totals ranging from 3 to 8 inches. Flash flooding from this rain closed several roads, including U.S. Highway 18 near New Hampton (Chickasaw County) and some homes sustained water damage. A train derailment southwest of New Hampton resulted from flooding along the Wapsipinicon River prompted officials to evacuate nearby residents when a tank car leaked anhydrous ammonia. The Chickasaw County Sheriff issued a no travel advisory for parts of the county because of the flooding. The Governor of Iowa issued a disaster proclamation for Allamakee, Chickasaw, Clayton, Floyd, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek Counties. A previous disaster proclamation was still in effect for Fayette County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down in Fort Atkinson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of storms rolled across portions of northeast Iowa during the afternoon of August 10th and caused some wind damage. A shed was rolled over in Fayette (Fayette County), trees were damaged in Clermont (Fayette County) and power lines were downed in West Union (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The metal roof was blown off an outbuilding on a farm south of Oran.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms rolled across northeast Iowa during the afternoon of August 24th. These storms produced winds strong enough to cause wind damage from Floyd County east across Chickasaw County into Fayette County. Trees were downed along U.S. Highway 63 near New Hampton (Chickasaw County) and 60 mph wind gusts in Oelwein (Fayette County) snapped and uprooted numerous trees and caused a building to partially collapse. One person was injured in Oelwein when they became trapped under the branches of a fallen tree. The Governor of Iowa issued a proclamation of disaster emergency for Fayette County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown over west of Charles City that also caused power lines to go down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along U.S. Highway 63 in Williamstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois and far northeast Missouri on Friday, June 18, and continued into the early morning hours of Saturday, June 19.  ||The most intense storms produced very large hail and damaging wind gusts.  These storms formed along a nearly stationary front that stretched across the southern half of the NWS Quad Cities forecast area.  Hail around 1.5 inches in diameter and tree damage were reported, especially in far southeast IA and northwestern IL.||Repeat thunderstorms also produced very heavy rainfall, with reports up to 4 inches. Due to the recent dry conditions, no high impact flash flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Young June corn flattened in an area around Milton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system moved across the Upper Midwest on December 15th and produced a rare severe weather outbreak across northeast Iowa. Eight tornadoes occurred across parts of Floyd, Chickasaw and Howard Counties. Four of these tornadoes were rated EF1 with one moving through Rudd (Floyd County) where the roof was removed from the library and several homes and a church were heavily damaged. The other tornadoes largely moved through rural areas damaging trees, power lines and farm buildings. In addition to the tornadoes, damaging winds occurred that blew over a semi in Floyd (Floyd County) and damaged numerous homes across the region. The Governor of Iowa declared a state of disaster emergency for Allamakee, Chickasaw, Fayette, Floyd, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek Counties. A federal disaster was declared for Floyd, Howard and Mitchell Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were snapped off, large branches blown down and a small farm outbuilding damaged northeast of Marble Rock.

In addition to the tornadoes, damaging winds occurred that blew over a semi in Floyd (Floyd County) and damaged numerous homes across the region. The Governor of Iowa declared a state of disaster emergency for Allamakee, Chickasaw, Fayette, Floyd, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek Counties. A federal disaster was declared for Floyd, Howard and Mitchell Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Farm outbuildings were damaged east of Elma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unprecedented storm system lifted across the central Plains from December 15 into the early hours of December 16. Kinematic fields were at the top of the climatological distributions, leading to extreme wind shear in the lowest levels. Ahead of the system, record high temperatures with surface dew point values into the 50s and lower 60s surged toward northwest Iowa and resulted in instability not typically found in December.||A serial derecho developed across Nebraska and Kansas during the early afternoon and raced to the northeast toward Iowa and Minnesota, with widespread damaging wind and a few tornadoes. The first Storm Prediction Center moderate risk, as well as severe thunderstorm and tornado warnings during December, were issued for the Sioux Falls warning area. There was even remaining snow cover in parts of northwest Iowa north of Highway 18 and into southwest Minnesota as the severe storms evolved during the afternoon and evening hours.||As the cold front swung through the area behind the convective line, 60 to 75 mph winds aloft were mixed to the surface and resulted in a few synoptic wind gusts up to hurricane force.  Some light wintry precipitation also fell during the transition toward the much colder air, which led to flash freezing as temperatures fell as much as 40 degrees during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 43 mph at station SBEI4 6 miles west southwest of Ocheyedan.

Ahead of the system, record high temperatures with surface dew point values into the 50s and lower 60s surged toward northwest Iowa and resulted in instability not typically found in December.||A serial derecho developed across Nebraska and Kansas during the early afternoon and raced to the northeast toward Iowa and Minnesota, with widespread damaging wind and a few tornadoes. The first Storm Prediction Center moderate risk, as well as severe thunderstorm and tornado warnings during December, were issued for the Sioux Falls warning area. There was even remaining snow cover in parts of northwest Iowa north of Highway 18 and into southwest Minnesota as the severe storms evolved during the afternoon and evening hours.||As the cold front swung through the area behind the convective line, 60 to 75 mph winds aloft were mixed to the surface and resulted in a few synoptic wind gusts up to hurricane force.  Some light wintry precipitation also fell during the transition toward the much colder air, which led to flash freezing as temperatures fell as much as 40 degrees during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was uprooted by thunderstorm winds.

Ahead of the system, record high temperatures with surface dew point values into the 50s and lower 60s surged toward northwest Iowa and resulted in instability not typically found in December.||A serial derecho developed across Nebraska and Kansas during the early afternoon and raced to the northeast toward Iowa and Minnesota, with widespread damaging wind and a few tornadoes. The first Storm Prediction Center moderate risk, as well as severe thunderstorm and tornado warnings during December, were issued for the Sioux Falls warning area. There was even remaining snow cover in parts of northwest Iowa north of Highway 18 and into southwest Minnesota as the severe storms evolved during the afternoon and evening hours.||As the cold front swung through the area behind the convective line, 60 to 75 mph winds aloft were mixed to the surface and resulted in a few synoptic wind gusts up to hurricane force.  Some light wintry precipitation also fell during the transition toward the much colder air, which led to flash freezing as temperatures fell as much as 40 degrees during the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large trees were downed by thunderstorm winds in Spencer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of supercell thunderstorms moved through the region beginning on the evening of June 14 and continuing into the early morning hours of June 15. The storms produced widespread wind gusts of 75 to 115 mph, hail 1 to 3 inches in diameter, and two EF1 tornadoes. Minor flooding was also reported in Omaha. The storms caused widespread tree damage, and some structural damage to homes and businesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported estimated wind gusts of 90 mph.

A few storms produced small hail with an ambulance in Des Moines reporting up to nickel sized hail. Behind the line of thunderstorms, environmental winds increased with gust of 50 to 60 mph reported in central Iowa. A couple sites gust just over 60 mph.

A deep surface low moved north of the area, setting up a tight pressure gradient in the southwest quadrant of the low across Iowa. Dry air allowed for deep mixing and gusty winds aloft to be mixed down. Winds were breezy much f the day, with many sites experiencing winds sustained at 40 mph and a few areas gusting over 60 mph.

A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops, powerlines, and structures along its path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed just west of Royal by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another shortwave moved across Iowa in early June, this one resulting in severe storms across southwest Iowa. Modest CAPE around 1000 J/kg was accompanied by deep layer shear that helped to organize storms and support rotating updrafts capable of large hail and damaging winds. By evening storms across southwest Iowa were producing up to golf ball sized hail and winds damaged several trees and outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS recorded a 67 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th. With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward. Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds damaged several outbuildings and downed several trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered storms moved across portions of northeast Iowa during the evening of July 4th. A few of these storms became strong enough to produce some hail across Fayette and Clayton Counties. The largest reported hail was quarter sized in Littleport (Clayton County) and Elkport (Clayton County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down east of Littleport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Iowa overnight from the 14th to 15th of June. The week had been steamy with near record highs and high humidity, making for a highly unstable airmass with CAPE over 3000 J/kg. Storms initiated along the front in central Iowa later on June 14th, resulting in a number of hail and wind damage reports. While storms dropped below severe limits shortly after midnight, heavy rain continued as thunderstorms trained along the nearly stationary front. This resulted in localized areas received two to four plus inches of rain that flooded a few area roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of a large tree on vehicle. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave pushed across the Midwest, resulting a a period of severe storms. the leading moisture axis and warm front were confined main to Minnesota in the afternoon, though a few stronger storms skimmed northern Iowa with a hail report in Winnebago county. The cold front passed across Iowa later in the evening from western to northern Iowa. Modest CAPE was in place but with strong deep layer shear over 40 kts and drier low levels noted in model soundings, gusty winds were the primary hazard with this second round of thunderstorms. Winds gusted over 60 mph with a few reports of damage to trees and outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several tree branches down on farmstead. Report via social media, time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough axis passed over the state and thunderstorms that had formed along a boundary in the early morning hours strengthened by midday. Despite relatively weak shear profiles, storms were able to organize enough to produce damaging wind gusts in Guthrie county. The storm complex continued into southeast Iowa into the afternoon, remaining below severe limits.||Rainfall in the Des Moines River basin was able to push the river, at Fleur Drive, briefly into flood stage on the 7th as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three outbuildings damaged and one destroyed. Time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms tracked east across Iowa Tuesday afternoon and evening bringing damaging wind, occasional lightning, and heavy rain to eastern IA and northwest IL.  The strongest reported winds of 60-70 mph, occurred in Buchanan and Linn counties.  These winds brought down trees and powerlines as they moved through in the evening.||A brief tornado occurred during the evening hours just southeast of Fairbank in an open field. Another brief tornado occurred just west of Winthrop IA in an open field.  Since no visible damage was observed with either tornado, they were assigned an EF-U rating.  The NWS would like to thank Buchanan County Emergency Management for assistance with the storm survey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated thunderstorm wind gusts of 60 to 70 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the approach of a strong mid-level wave, a cluster of multicellular storms evolved within a weakly unstable and weakly-sheared environment.  A fair amount of dry air at lower levels made for 600 J/kg or more of DCAPE, with storms producing some spotty significant wind damage along a track from near Paullina to around Primghar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree branches were downed by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified and moved from the Central Plains into Minnesota. After initially warm thermal profiles resulted in mainly rain or a brief mix, infiltration of colder air and the change to light snow resulted in minor accumulations mainly to the north and west. However, northwest Iowa did not escape strong northwest winds which reached a gusty 40 to 60 mph, and many areas found considerable drifting of earlier snowfall along and north of Iowa Highway 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest wind briefly gusted to 58 mph at the Automated Surface Observing System site KSUX near Sioux City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough across the Rockies underwent lee cyclogenesis, pulling a warm front north into Iowa in the afternoon. While a few storms developed along the warm front, most development was along the later cold front that moved across the area in the evening. These storms were frequent hail producers, with many reports of baseball sized hail or larger. A tornado also occurred which resulted in damage to a few homes, out buildings, and trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marshalltown ASOS measured 58 mph gust behind storms in a developing wake low.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Linear storms developed near a frontal boundary, moving across southeast South Dakota and into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota early on June 24. Within an environment of shear, a strong low-level jet, and strong DCAPE, some storms produced severe-level winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts downed several tree branches up to 3 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms tracked across southeast Iowa during the mid-afternoon into early evening of June 24, producing large hail up to golf ball size in Van Buren County and tennis ball size hail near Ottumwa.||More organized clusters of severe storms tracked through Dubuque into far northwest IL during the evening and late night hours of June 24.  Another storm cluster moved through the Quad Cities, producing wind gusts to 70 mph at the Davenport ASOS and damage to trees and power poles.  An EF-0 tornado also tracked east along and north of I-80 near the Davenport Airport, causing sporadic minor tree and structure damage.  This storm impacted the Quad City Airshow grounds, while no visitors were present.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media post by local media, of a damaged outbuilding and corn crib.

Another storm cluster moved through the Quad Cities, producing wind gusts to 70 mph at the Davenport ASOS and damage to trees and power poles.  An EF-0 tornado also tracked east along and north of I-80 near the Davenport Airport, causing sporadic minor tree and structure damage.  This storm impacted the Quad City Airshow grounds, while no visitors were present.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees reported blown down in Bettendorf.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Thursday, June 29, a powerful severe wind event moved over the region. This event was known as a derecho given its long duration from north of Kansas City to southern Indiana and its consistent strong intensity. Almost all of the damage in the area surveyed locally was due to straight-line winds, at times easily peaking between 100-120 mph in far western Illinois. Those who experienced the event conveyed how the strongest winds persisted for 10 to 15 minutes, and in some locations, it lasted as long as 20 minutes, which is atypical for derecho events.||There were some swaths of enhanced damage. These were largely due to intense wind surges in the local area. One of these in Clark county, Mo was determined to be a short-lived EF1 tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power line were reported down, with estimated 70 to 80 mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system slowly dropping SE form the eastern Dakotas and SW Minnesota dragged a cold front across the state during the evening and overnight hours of the 24th of June. Within the warm sector, MUCAPE values peaked in excess of 3000 J/kg during peak heating and scaled back a bit overnight but remained around or in excess of 2000 J/kg. Effective shear values were supportive for organized convection, residing in the 35 to around 50 kt range. Morning and lingering convective activity likely played a role in initiating some storms with remnant outflow boundaries around. As a result, a number of isolated or clustered organized storms were seen during the evening and overnight hours and produced a number of reports of large hail and damaging winds. The largest hail reported was 3 inches in both Palo Alto and Black Hawk counties. A couple of funnel clouds were reported as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KWWL relayed images of uprooted tree and gazebo damage. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak impulse dropped southward along the western side of a Great Lakes closed mid-level low, igniting isolated storms overnight. Weak shear and evaporation within a low-level dry airmass resulted in localized damaging wind gusts with the strongest storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees and a grain bin were damaged by thunderstorm winds. Property and crop damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong system developed on the morning of March 31st and tracked across the state of Iowa. This system pulled unseasonably warm and moist air into the Midwest, which combined with a very favorable wind profile to create a volatile environment for severe thunderstorms.  Discrete supercell thunderstorms occurred initially, but quickly transitioned into a QLCS mode. This system produced a historic tornado outbreak for the area, with a record 29 tornadoes on the day, including 13 rated EF-2, 1 rated EF-3, and 1 rated EF-4. This was the first EF-4 tornado in the NWS Quad Cities service area since 1995. 11 injuries were reported, with no known fatalities. In addition to the numerous tornadoes, there were many large hail and damaging wind reports. Maximum straight line thunderstorm winds were estimated at 80 to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated thunderstorm wind gusts of 60 to 70 MPH with no damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level wave across northeastern South Dakota ignited isolated afternoon thunderstorms near a cold front across south central South Dakota. Storms thrived and became briefly supercellular in a highly sheared and unstable environment while tracking eastward south of South Dakota Highway 46 into the early evening. After a brief decrease in intensity entering Iowa, storms strengthened once again mid to late evening moving east of Cherokee generally along and south of Iowa Highway 3 with a variety of severe weather types.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage was done by strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave traveling across the region, paired with modest MUCAPE and moisture above the midlevels allowed for sub-severe to severe hail across portions of Northern Iowa through the late evening. Large hail of 2-3 inches occurred in Humboldt and Hancock counties with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of trees down near the intersection of 160th and Noble Avenue which was time estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front with an associated surface low pressure system pushed into the state on the afternoon of September 23rd. Favorable instability and bulk shear present in the atmosphere allowed for strong to severe storms, which produced some sub-severe to severe hail and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report of damage to an outbuilding. Time of damage was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms moved across the area bringing hail and damaging wind gusts to central Iowa. The first round moved across the area around midday as remnants of an MCS out of Kansas and Nebraska moved into the area. By afternoon a front lifted across the area and afternoon heating allowed for thunderstorm redevelopment, becoming severe with MLCAPE exceeding 2000 J/kg and increasing deep layer shear. Later in the evening a line of thunderstorms moved out of Nebraska and into Iowa, sustained by a strong low-level jet. This bowing line of storms was mostly sub severe, but did result in thunderstorm wind damage late in eastern portions of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured wind gust reported by the Pella AWOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet surging into the region, paired with increasing warm air and moisture advection into the morning hours of May 24th, led to the development of a line of showers and storms (QLCS) in Nebraska, which then swept across Iowa. MUCAPE values around 2000J/kg ahead of this line allowed for these storms to become severe within the QLCS as it rapidly tracked eastward across Iowa through the morning. Severe hail occurred with these storms, with values generally between 1-1.75 inches, though hail up to 2.5-2.75 inches was reported in Wayne county. Outside of the hail, severe winds were the primary hazard, with values reported generally between 58-75 mph. Widespread damage was reported due to the winds, with the majority of this damage being snapped trees/branches and power poles, some falling onto structures, as well as blown over semis. Outside of the hail and winds, a handful of tornadoes also occurred (please refer to the separate entry for those details).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report of a large tree down south of Indianola. Time of occurrence was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a line of storms producing damaging winds and tornadoes passed across the area in the morning, a second wave passed over the area. The wave lacked strong forcing and present weak instability, but deep layer shear was high. At the same time, moisture fueling storm development did not make it closer to the surface and low levels were dry. This allowed for rapid evaporative cooling as precipitation fell, producing gusty winds with what appeared as rather weak storms on radar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snapped Tree branches, roughly 4 to 5 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remains of an MCV that tracked across Nebraska earlier in the day moved into Iowa in the late afternoon. These remnants moved into a deeply unstable environment with 2000+ J/kg of SBCAPE and intensified through the early evening, transitioning to a loosely organized bowing segment in the evening. Steep mid and low level lapse rates and DCAPE of 750-1000 J/kg were also available. This combination resulted in a handful of hail and damaging wind gusts across the area.  Heavy rain also occurred as a line of training convection developed behind the bowing segment lasting late into the night. A swath of 3 to 5 inches of rain occurred with the system in central to southern Iowa, however no flood impacts were noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds with large tree branch down. Time estimated by social media time and radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A PV anomaly entering the region and associated surface boundary interactions, with increasing forcing overhead and favorable deep layer shear, resulted in scattered on and off showers and thunderstorms throughout much of the day. This resulted in a handful of severe wind reports and isolated wind damage, particularly in Clarke and Marshall counties with gusts generally ranging between 60 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No damage has been reported with this wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms redeveloped near an area of low pressure and advancing frontal boundary across northwest Iowa. Storms in the dry slot produced gusty wind while organizing into a bowing line, with several reports of brief funnel clouds and one location along the line spinning up a weak tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several power poles were leaning due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly unstable air mass gained ground near and to the east of an inverted trough which locked in place from central Nebraska into southwest MN.  An increase in warm advection into the region along with slightly stronger deep-layer effective shear up to 35 knots allowed for scattered and fairly discrete storms to develop, some of which became supercellular with very large hail. Hail up to 3 inches in diameter fell, with a few storms producing winds in excess of 70 mph.

A large scale bow echo developed across Nebraska in the late night hours and pushed northeast toward the lower Missouri River valley and northwest Iowa.  Winds associated with the convective line were as high as around 70 mph in the Sioux City metro area, and caused considerable tree damage and power outages to around 2000 customers. The roof on one home in the Morningside area of Sioux City was damaged by a falling tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees and large branches along Floyd Boulevard.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Powerline down and an out building destroyed. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong negatively tilted trough lifted northward through the Western Plains during the afternoon and evening of May 6. While instability was not intense with around 1000 J/kg MLCAPE, a very strong low-level jet produced a considerable amount of boundary parallel deep-layer shear to entice a quick linear upscale growth, but the southwest-oriented shear in the 0-3km layer also led to brief mesovortices with enhanced wind corridors in segments which became oriented more northwest to southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant roof damage to outbuildings was caused by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of severe storms impacted Iowa on July 30th. This first round occurred through the early morning as a bowing line of storms moved into the area from Nebraska around 1 am CDT. This passed across the area through the early morning hours, exiting into southeastern Iowa by 5 am. Several sites reported wind gusts of 60-65 mph as this line of storms swept across the area. A second bowing segment passed across the are in the mid to late morning, this time bringing hail along with the winds. In its wake, thunderstorms developed on the outflow boundary across central to southeast Iowa. These storms were slow moving and produced heavy rain along with a number of quarter sized and larger hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Personal weather station recorded a 66 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of severe storms impacted Iowa on July 30th. This first round occurred through the early morning as a bowing line of storms moved into the area from Nebraska around 1 am CDT. This passed across the area through the early morning hours, exiting into southeastern Iowa by 5 am. Several sites reported wind gusts of 60-65 mph as this line of storms swept across the area. A second bowing segment passed across the are in the mid to late morning, this time bringing hail along with the winds. In its wake, thunderstorms developed on the outflow boundary across central to southeast Iowa. These storms were slow moving and produced heavy rain along with a number of quarter sized and larger hail reports.

A large scale bow echo developed across Nebraska in the late night hours and pushed northeast toward the lower Missouri River valley and northwest Iowa.  Winds associated with the convective line were as high as around 70 mph in the Sioux City metro area, and caused considerable tree damage and power outages to around 2000 customers. The roof on one home in the Morningside area of Sioux City was damaged by a falling tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust was measured by the Automated Surface Observing System site KSUX at Sioux Gateway Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following morning convection over central and southern Iowa tied to a system further south into Missouri, a frontal boundary sweeping across northern into the eastern parts of Iowa by the afternoon to evening resulted in additional showers and thunderstorms. The parameter space overhead included MLCAPE values of 1000+ J/kg and deep layer shear around 40-50+ knots. In particular, Cerro Gordo county received severe thunderstorm wind damage, with estimated gusts around 58 mph. Otherwise, no other reports were received for this particular event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report of large limbs approximately 6-8 inches in diameter down on a home and power lines in Ventura. Time of occurrence estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front pushed across Iowa the evening of June 18th, with rain lingering into the early morning of June 19th. Storms initially began as discrete cells in western Iowa, growing upscale by early evening as they moved west. The increasing LLJ was enough to fuel storms into the overnight, and provided a supply of moisture to contribute to severe storm development and heavy rain. While not widespread, the line of storms did produce a few damaging gusts in north central Iowa, along with a swath of two to three plus inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree branch down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved across the midwest, pulling a surface boundary across the state. In the afternoon weak convection developed along this boundary, but most storms tended to be short-lived in nature with relatively weak shear profiles. These storms resulted in mainly wind damage reports across east-central Iowa. Convection continued to bubble through the evening with continued wind damage reports. Shortly after midnight convection out of the Dakotas made it to northern Iowa. This broken line of storms pushed across the state all night, with essentially no breaks in reports of large hail and damaging winds through sunrise. Additionally, some locations received heavy rain. Storms cleared the Des Moines metro area shortly after five in the morning and diminished in strength shortly after that.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter report of tree branches down with winds estimated at 60 to 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low to the northwest of Iowa allowed for convection to develop in the warm sector and along the surface boundary as it pushed across northern Iowa through the afternoon and evening of the 21st. These storms caused damaging winds and large hail across northern and central Iowa. Localized areas received heavy rain that resulted in flooding overnight. Another shortwave embedded within the parent longwave trough allowed for additional rain across the state on the 22nd that lasted through the afternoon. This second round of rain exacerbated the ongoing flooded areas, resulting in flooding that lingered into the morning of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jasper county dispatch relayed reports of tree damage between Newton and Reasnor. Other tree damage in eastern Jasper county. Time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated supercell developed behind a southward sagging frontal boundary.  With around 1000 J/kg MLCAPE and 40-45 knots of effective shear, a brief supercellular structure evolved. Even with hail size somewhat limited by available instability, substantial damage to total destruction of crops occurred due to the large quantity of wind-driven hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds snapped off a one-foot diameter tree a few feet above the ground. Property damage costs are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly station boundary draped itself across northern Iowa, oriented east-west on the evening of the 27th and allowed isolated supercells and multi cellular storms to initiate and in some cases train/develop over the same area for a number of hours. In total, severe weather (damaging winds, funnel clouds, tornadoes), heavy rain, and flooding occurred. ||The portions of northern Iowa that experienced the severe and hazardous weather resided just south of the surface boundary and included the following environment: SB/MUCAPE in excess of 2000 j/kg, LCLs under 1000m, effective bulk shear around/in excess of 30 kts, precipitable water values in excess of 1.8 inches, supercell composite values in excess of 12, effective SRH around 200 m2/s2, and 0-3km SRH around 200-300 m2/s2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small tree limbs down at one to three inches in diameter. Power line was knocked down as well. Report via picture on social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level shortwave trough provided the focus for storm development late in the evening of August 30th. Initial disorganized convection across northern Iowa became more organized moving across central and into southern Iowa where better instability and steep lapse rates allowed deeper convection. Storms grew upscale into a bowing line that produced damaging gusts from 55 to 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Second hand, delayed report of wind damage from just north of Vail to east of Deloit. Hog barn roof blown off, several trees uprooted, and some hail damage. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of strong thunderstorms formed in northeast Missouri early in the morning, August 11th, 2021.  These storms expanded and moved northeast into northern Illinois, with a swath of damaging winds, hail, and very heavy rainfall. Some flash flooding was reported in southeast Iowa. As the storms approached the Mississippi River, a large swath of strong winds developed behind the storms, resulting in sustained winds of 40 to 60 mph, and damage through the Quad Cities metro area. This type of event is due to a wake low pressure system, behind the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County EMA reported widespread winds of 40 to 60 mph through the county, with areas of tree limb damage, and minor property damage such as trampolines blown over.  These winds occurred after the storm had ended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system was centered over Kansas and Nebraska with attendant warm front draped across north central Iowa. This was bisected by an outflow boundary from morning convection across western Iowa. As morning activity exited the are the atmosphere quickly recovered steep low and mid level lapse rates exceeded 8 C/km with 0-6 km shear over 40 knots. Near the remnant outflow boundary 0-1 km SRH exceeded 150 m2/s2. Storms developed along this boundary and moved east along the warm front, spawning a number of tornadoes across the state. In fact, this is in the top 5 for tornadoes occurring in a single day for the state of Iowa since reliable records began in the 1980s. By evening convection congealed into an MCS, bringing heavy rain to portions of the state. A line of training storms set up north of the Des Moines metro, resulting in localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms initially developed across Nebraska along a cold front and aided by the low level jet before spreading across Iowa after midnight on the morning of August 8th. While instability was more than sufficient at 2500-3000 J/kg, shear prevent further organization and maintained an MCS storm mode. A small hail report and brief strong wind gust occurred with some sites getting two to five inches of rain, the storms generally remained below severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported minor tree damage with one large limb on a car and power lines. Time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Preexisting outflow boundaries from previous storms and a weak and slow moving cold front al boundary provided opportunities for storms to develop in the early morning hours on the 25th. Overall, the environment was not supportive for organized convection with limited effective bulk shear and elevated nature, but was able to produce a lone thunderstorm wind damage report (trees down). Additionally, precipitable water values in excess of 1.8 inches and slow moving and/or training nature of storms allowed areas to receive heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three trees downed, blocking northbound lanes on Iowa 137. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois and far northeast Missouri on Friday, June 18, and continued into the early morning hours of Saturday, June 19.  ||The most intense storms produced very large hail and damaging wind gusts.  These storms formed along a nearly stationary front that stretched across the southern half of the NWS Quad Cities forecast area.  Hail around 1.5 inches in diameter and tree damage were reported, especially in far southeast IA and northwestern IL.||Repeat thunderstorms also produced very heavy rainfall, with reports up to 4 inches. Due to the recent dry conditions, no high impact flash flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down, blocking the road at teh intersection of 46th Street and L Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across Iowa, Illinois and Missouri on Sunday, June 20th. Early in the morning, an MCS from the night before moved into our area along a stationary boundary and brought widespread rain showers to the area. This died off early in the day and then some clear skies moved in, providing some surface heating ahead of an approaching cold front. Ahead of this front, several discrete supercell thunderstorms formed, which posed a threat for tornadoes, hail, and severe wind gusts. Later in the day. these supercells generally grew upscale into a line of thunderstorms with embedded supercell structures. A tornado was spotted near Bernard, Iowa and lead to some damage to trees and farm outbuildings. Otherwise, severe winds reports and a few hail reports resulted from these storms. The severe threat continued eastward, and heavily impacted some suburbs of Chicago.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across Iowa, Illinois and Missouri on Sunday, June 20th. Early in the morning, an MCS from the night before moved into our area along a stationary boundary and brought widespread rain showers to the area. This died off early in the day and then some clear skies moved in, providing some surface heating ahead of an approaching cold front. Ahead of this front, several discrete supercell thunderstorms formed, which posed a threat for tornadoes, hail, and severe wind gusts. Later in the day. these supercells generally grew upscale into a line of thunderstorms with embedded supercell structures. A tornado was spotted near Bernard, Iowa and lead to some damage to trees and farm outbuildings. Otherwise, severe winds reports and a few hail reports resulted from these storms. The severe threat continued eastward, and heavily impacted some suburbs of Chicago.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few large limbs blown down, along with the roof blown off a baseball clubhouse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept across the area on Friday March 25th, with strong gusty winds developing behind the front. There were widespread wind speeds of 20 to 30 MPH with gusts up to 45 MPH. Rain showers developed behind the cold front with reported wind gusts in these showers of 50 to 60 MPH during the afternoon and evening hours. There was a wind gust of 67 mph at the Cedar Rapids Airport. Winds began to diminish after 10 PM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Cedar Rapids Airport recorded a wind gust of 67 mph, which was brief and occurred with a nearby rain shower. The FAA tower reported a gust of 54 knots measured by other equipment at the same time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening midlevel trough over the central Rockies advected north an unstable airmass into Nebraska and Iowa. Storms initiated to the northeast of the triple point leading to two tornadoes and numerous hail and strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: IADOT sensor measured a peak wind gust of 76 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early morning convection led to a few strong thunderstorm wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter measured a peak wind gust of 52 kts and reported power out in Essex.

Dry air allowed for deep mixing and gusty winds aloft to be mixed down. Winds were breezy much f the day, with many sites experiencing winds sustained at 40 mph and a few areas gusting over 60 mph.

Showers and thunderstorms developed along a cold front in the early morning hours, with quarter sized hail and a few stronger winds gusts reported in central Iowa around sunrise. This pushed east of the area by mid morning and temperatures warmed. This allowed for deep mixing and non-thunderstorm associated wind gusts of 50 to over 60 mph occurred across the area through the afternoon. In the evening a secondary front passed across the state, with daytime heating allowing for more robust convection to develop. The bowing, linear storm system primarily produced a swath of wind gusts and damage, however a few smaller hail reports were made.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS report of a 59 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs, but strong deep-layer forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds dislodged locked machine shed doors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure developed across central South Dakota on Saturday, which moved slowly toward northern Minnesota by Sunday morning. Southerly winds gusted as high as 50 to 70 mph at times in the late morning and afternoon, which caused sporadic tree and building damage across the area. A considerable amount of blowing dust also occurred with the strongest winds, with visibility briefly a mile or less. The strong winds combined with very low humidity and dormant vegetation resulted in numerous wildfires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 43 mph at times during the afternoon at a personal weather station 2 miles west of Terril, with a peak wind gust of 58 mph at 1244CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure continued to produce a strong gradient across the Northern Plains, which intensified with another pulse of cold advection on April 14. Numerous high-profile vehicles had difficulty navigating the extreme wind gusts, with a few overturned semi-trucks across northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds were frequently between 40 and 44 mph at Iowa State Mesonet site DONI4 3 miles east-northeast of Doon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered storms moved across northeast Iowa during the evening of August 27th. A few of these storms produced some strong wind gusts with trees blown down in Castalia (Winneshiek County), near Jackson Junction (Winneshiek County), and east of Decorah (Winneshiek County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Castalia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front pushed across Iowa the evening of June 18th, with rain lingering into the early morning of June 19th. Storms initially began as discrete cells in western Iowa, growing upscale by early evening as they moved west. The increasing LLJ was enough to fuel storms into the overnight, and provided a supply of moisture to contribute to severe storm development and heavy rain. While not widespread, the line of storms did produce a few damaging gusts in north central Iowa, along with a swath of two to three plus inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree damage and minor building damage with a small portion of a barn roof removed.

The increasing LLJ was enough to fuel storms into the overnight, and provided a supply of moisture to contribute to severe storm development and heavy rain. While not widespread, the line of storms did produce a few damaging gusts in north central Iowa, along with a swath of two to three plus inches of rain.

Scattered convection occurred throughout the day across the area as a weak cold front dropped slowly south across the area. By afternoon the front had largely stalled near the I-80 corridor and more vigorous convection developed along the front, fed by high instability exceeding 3000 J/kg and steep mid level lapse rates of 7 to 8 C/km. Storms first began near Newton and then back built towards the west along the boundary through the evening. Storms produced large hail and stretching resulted in a huge number of funnel clouds, including pictures with a least visible at the same time. Later in the evening a wave across Nebraska moved into Iowa, sweeping east along the instability gradient that the earlier evening convection developed on. A few damaging wind gusts occurred with this line of storms, however it remained sub severe most of the night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines down. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCV lifting into eastern Iowa into the early afternoon, with favorable conditions led to funnel cloud formation in eastern Iowa, particularly near the Tama/Poweshiek county border. Following this activity, an upper level trough and associated cold front entered western Iowa into the afternoon and pushed across the state, with 1000-2000 J/kg of MLCAPE and shear around 20-30 kts allowing for some storms to become severe. This led to some damaging winds and hail to occur in portions of central Iowa, as well as heavy rain in central and northern Iowa given the slow-moving nature of the storms.  A brief tornado occurred with thunderstorms northwest of Fort Dodge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report of tree downed on some lines at the intersection of 14th and Story St. Large branch down also at 14th and Boone St. Time of occurrence estimated from radar.

More isolated afternoon and early evening storms erupted just north of a frontal boundary across northeast Nebraska and western Iowa, which produced up to quarter size hail and damaging wind gusts of 60 to 75 mph. Storms became more widespread during the evening given a mid-level impulse and increase in the low-level jet across the frontal boundary. The rain regenerated in many of the same areas as the previous night's excessive rainfall and brought two-day totals to as high as 8 to 16 inches setting several records. Widespread flash flooding developed during the evening and overnight hours with catastrophic damage in Rock Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Campers were tossed as well as buildings and trees damaged, including to a fireworks stand, as thunderstorm outflow winds pushed through the east side of Sheldon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a morning mesoscale convective system (MCS) across Nebraska, storms refired during the evening hours across northwest Iowa within fairly weak deep-layer shear and up to 2000 J/kg MUCAPE. Storms produced locally damaging wind as cores collapsed, and training of storms resulted in locally heavy rainfall of 2 to 3 inches around the Iowa Great Lakes in the matter of an hour or two.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds damaged an outbuilding and dented in a grain bin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed along a boundary draped across northern Iowa on the afternoon of September 19th with additional storms through the evening on a cold front that moved across the area later in the evening. With over 2000 J/kg of available MLCAPE, steep lapse rates, and modestly curved hodographs that supported rotating updrafts, hail was reported in several of the storms that developed. Storms later into the evening produced wind damage in parts of southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report of a tree down north of Reasnor. Report via social media and time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave passed across the area, bringing widespread rain with over two inches falling in much of southeast Iowa. By afternoon weak instability developed, and when combined with robust available shear, was enough to produce isolated hail and damaging wind gusts. Storms organized into a QLCS in the evening and while hodographs were supportive of rotating updrafts and the potential for tornadoes, storms remained just elevated and prevented any further development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Personal weather station recorded a 60 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late in the evening of August 15th, an area of storms moved through southeast Iowa and west central Illinois. These brought large hail and damaging winds to portions of the area, along with heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed late in the day on Aug 27th in northeast Missouri and spread into southeast Iowa and far western Illinois into the evening. Large hail up to golf ball size fell for nearly one half hour straight in Rutledge, MO.||A pronounced downburst (powerful winds that descend from a thunderstorm and spread out quickly once they hit the ground) caused widespread damage in Burlington, IA, knocking down trees, limbs, and powerlines. Five homes had holes in the roofs due to falling limbs. A commercial storage building had a partial roof collapse and another large tall outbuilding had partial roofing peeled back, consistent with wind estimates of at least 80 mph. ||Rainfall totals of 2 to 4 inches were observed in parts of northeast Missouri and southeast Iowa. ||Oppressive heat and humidity that began on the 26th and persisted into the 27th helped spawn these storms. Both Dubuque (tied) and Moline (exceeded) daily record highs on the 27th, with Moline reaching 98�� and Dubuque 94��.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated 60 MPH winds with heavy rain.

A commercial storage building had a partial roof collapse and another large tall outbuilding had partial roofing peeled back, consistent with wind estimates of at least 80 mph. ||Rainfall totals of 2 to 4 inches were observed in parts of northeast Missouri and southeast Iowa. ||Oppressive heat and humidity that began on the 26th and persisted into the 27th helped spawn these storms. Both Dubuque (tied) and Moline (exceeded) daily record highs on the 27th, with Moline reaching 98�� and Dubuque 94��.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported by Burlington airport ASOS station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure developed across central South Dakota on Saturday, which moved slowly toward northern Minnesota by Sunday morning. Southerly winds gusted as high as 50 to 70 mph at times in the late morning and afternoon, which caused sporadic tree and building damage across the area. A considerable amount of blowing dust also occurred with the strongest winds, with visibility briefly a mile or less. The strong winds combined with very low humidity and dormant vegetation resulted in numerous wildfires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 48 mph at times during the late morning and afternoon at Automated Weather Observing System site KSXK near Maurice, with a peak wind gust of 63 mph at 1215CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another shortwave moved across Iowa in early June, this one resulting in severe storms across southwest Iowa. Modest CAPE around 1000 J/kg was accompanied by deep layer shear that helped to organize storms and support rotating updrafts capable of large hail and damaging winds. By evening storms across southwest Iowa were producing up to golf ball sized hail and winds damaged several trees and outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pine trees uprooted and split. Damage to an outbuilding including some broken windows.

The strongest reported winds of 60-70 mph, occurred in Buchanan and Linn counties.  These winds brought down trees and powerlines as they moved through in the evening.||A brief tornado occurred during the evening hours just southeast of Fairbank in an open field. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th. With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward. Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts brought down numerous large tree limbs, with several trees snapped or uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops, powerlines, and structures along its path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and power lines downed by thunderstorm winds in George.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave pushed across the Midwest, resulting a a period of severe storms. the leading moisture axis and warm front were confined main to Minnesota in the afternoon, though a few stronger storms skimmed northern Iowa with a hail report in Winnebago county. The cold front passed across Iowa later in the evening from western to northern Iowa. Modest CAPE was in place but with strong deep layer shear over 40 kts and drier low levels noted in model soundings, gusty winds were the primary hazard with this second round of thunderstorms. Winds gusted over 60 mph with a few reports of damage to trees and outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down in neighborhood with power outage. Delayed report, time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low-pressure system which had been moving slowly off the lee side of the Rocky Mountains had finally progressed into central Nebraska. This left western Iowa in the warm sector as a warm front moved north into the area. The SPC issued an Enhanced Risk for severe weather with a 30% hatched for severe hail. The severe weather mostly stayed west of the Missouri River. In Iowa, there was one report for a severe wind gust. The most impactful weather was a line of training storms that lead to flash flooding in Harrison County that caused significant damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported via mPing of 1-inch tree limbs broken; shingles blown off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread significant damaging wind storm tracked across nearly all of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa on the morning of July 12. These storms produced widespread winds of 60 to 80 mph, with embedded stronger downbursts with wind speeds up to 100 mph. The strongest measured wind gust was 82 mph in Dodge County NE, but some of the high-end straight line wind damage supports embedded wind speeds up to 100 mph.||This storm also produced a few embedded tornadoes. Some of the tornado tracks were only identified by narrow swaths of damage through mature cornfields, with minimal damage otherwise and peak wind speeds similar to the nearby downburst winds. All but one of the tornadoes was preceded by a Wireless Emergency Alert. There were 0 reported injuries or fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported a semi blown over and part of roof blown off a building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across western Iowa which had observed unseasonably warm temperatures in the past couple of days. The front set off a line of storms that produced strong wind gusts and smaller hail. The strongest measured wind gust was 68 mph in Page County. A flash flood was also reported in Pottawattamie County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public personal weather station recorded a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection began in early afternoon in eastern Iowa along a subtle boundary across the state. This was fueled by high CAPE exceeding 3000 J/kg with deep layer shear sufficient to support large hail growth. Later in the evening a secondary round of convection moved across the area producing additional hail in the form of a cold pool driven MCS, driven along the plume of higher instability. The result was dozen of hail reports up to baseball sized across much of central and southern Iowa, damaging homes and vehicles and taking down power lines. As storms crossed Interstate 80 an accident resulted in significant delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated severe straight line winds with minor flying debris near the I-35 and Highway 34 interchange.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong system developed on the morning of March 31st and tracked across the state of Iowa. This system pulled unseasonably warm and moist air into the Midwest, which combined with a very favorable wind profile to create a volatile environment for severe thunderstorms.  Discrete supercell thunderstorms occurred initially, but quickly transitioned into a QLCS mode. This system produced a historic tornado outbreak for the area, with a record 29 tornadoes on the day, including 13 rated EF-2, 1 rated EF-3, and 1 rated EF-4. This was the first EF-4 tornado in the NWS Quad Cities service area since 1995. 11 injuries were reported, with no known fatalities. In addition to the numerous tornadoes, there were many large hail and damaging wind reports. Maximum straight line thunderstorm winds were estimated at 80 to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured gust by the AWOS at the Muscatine Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large upper level ridging was in place for several days in late May to early June. With little movement in the Great Lakes area surface high, Iowa experiences afternoon thunderstorms largely driven by good moisture transport and diurnal heating each day. On the afternoon of June 2nd, a couple storms manages to strengthen more than the others scattered across the area, producing damaging wind gusts in Des Moines and Marshalltown, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report of a large, healthy tree snapped at the trunk near Ferguson. Estimated sized was 2.5 feet in diameter. Report via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level wave across northeastern South Dakota ignited isolated afternoon thunderstorms near a cold front across south central South Dakota. Storms thrived and became briefly supercellular in a highly sheared and unstable environment while tracking eastward south of South Dakota Highway 46 into the early evening. After a brief decrease in intensity entering Iowa, storms strengthened once again mid to late evening moving east of Cherokee generally along and south of Iowa Highway 3 with a variety of severe weather types.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power pole along 80th Avenue just south of Iowa Highway 3 was leaning, nearby corn was flattened, and large tree branches were broken due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot and humid air in place across eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and far northeast Missouri interacted with a southward moving frontal boundary and disturbance aloft, setting the stage for development and organization of severe thunderstorms across the region. Storms initially formed from north central Iowa and southern Wisconsin along the frontal boundary, which slowly worked south into the hot, humid air in place across the region. As storms arrived across east central Iowa, they began producing winds around 70 MPH across the Hwy 20 corridor. Another storm to the south further intensified, and began producing winds of 80+ MPH as it tracked along the Hwy 30 corridor. This impacted the northern suburbs of Cedar Rapids into Clinton. These storms continued to produce wind gusts around 60-70 MPH as they progressed into northwest Illinois. One brief EF-0 tornado began in Clinton, Iowa, then moved across the Mississippi River before dissipating near Fulton, Illinois. Damage near Andrew, Iowa in Jackson County was determined to be straight-line winds. Storms additionally produced large hail around the Dubuque metro, with quarter size hail reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and large branches downed by the wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching upper level jet within the presence of a sharp lower- to mid-level boundary focused widespread convective development during the overnight hours. Elevated instability of 1000-1500 J/kg and deep layer shear in excess of 40 kts produced a very organized bowing system, which resulted in widespread 60 to 80 mph wind reports in southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa. At the peak, several thousand people were without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were downed by thunderstorm winds in Hawarden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot and humid air in place across eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and far northeast Missouri interacted with a southward moving frontal boundary and disturbance aloft, setting the stage for development and organization of severe thunderstorms across the region. Storms initially formed from north central Iowa and southern Wisconsin along the frontal boundary, which slowly worked south into the hot, humid air in place across the region. As storms arrived across east central Iowa, they began producing winds around 70 MPH across the Hwy 20 corridor. Another storm to the south further intensified, and began producing winds of 80+ MPH as it tracked along the Hwy 30 corridor. This impacted the northern suburbs of Cedar Rapids into Clinton. These storms continued to produce wind gusts around 60-70 MPH as they progressed into northwest Illinois. One brief EF-0 tornado began in Clinton, Iowa, then moved across the Mississippi River before dissipating near Fulton, Illinois. Damage near Andrew, Iowa in Jackson County was determined to be straight-line winds. Storms additionally produced large hail around the Dubuque metro, with quarter size hail reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs blown down, with shingles blown off. Report from mping.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front across South Dakota and Minnesota dropped south across Iowa on the evening of July 10th. Environmental parameters were marginal with CAPE around 1000 J/kg and deep layer shear around 20 to 25 knots. Convergence along the front allowed for storms to develop in the late afternoon, moving across northern and northwest Iowa. Storms strengthened to severe criteria across Emmet county, where hail up to baseball sized was reported along with a wind gust of 58 mph at the Estherville ASOS.

An outflow boundary from earlier day convection focused a gradient of instability and storm development with additional destabilization from heating by early afternoon. Boundary interactions from sluggish storms led to training of heavy rainfall cores, as well as a couple brief tornadoes. Extensive flooding resulted in a state disaster declaration for Sioux County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust to 71 mph was measured by a law enforcement officer.  Some minor damage to shingles and trees was observed near the point the wind was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe thunderstorms developed across much of eastern Iowa on the evening of May 6, 2023. Storms mainly produced hail up to golf ball size. Through the evening, storms merged and weakened in intensity, but not before producing some wind damage across northern and central Lee County. An isolated tornado also occurred in Muscatine County. Heavy rain also fell from storms as some remained stationary for long periods of time. Most notably, locations north and east of Fairfield, Iowa recorded over two inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Burlington Airport measured a 60 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At midnight on the 16th, non-severe thunderstorms were ongoing across portions of northeast Nebraska and western Iowa. With these storms being rooted in a nearly stationary boundary co-located with the exit region of a low-level jet, this area saw prolonged periods of moderate to heavy rain. ||A second round of scattered thunderstorms brought heavy rain and gusty winds between 7 and 11 in the morning. These storms were non-severe as well. By the afternoon, the cut-off low in central Nebraska began to drift to the northeast. A region of light to moderate rain moved into southwest Iowa after 3 pm, likely enhancing surface winds by bringing down higher momentum flow aloft. This resulted in strong non-thunderstorm winds across southwest Iowa including one severe wind gust recorded on a personal weather station.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe wind gust was measured by a private weather station shortly after 4 in the afternoon.
