EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system developed across the eastern Great Plains and Midwest during the afternoon of November 26, and brought a strong cold front across the area during the evening and overnight hours. Several rounds of showers and strong thunderstorms develop along this front, some of which produced severe wind gusts. Damage was limited mainly to trees, and mainly occurred over southeast Iowa and west central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported estimated thunderstorm winds between 55 to 65 MPH along Highway 61 near Keokuk. The time of the event was estimated by the spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system and its attendant cold front moved across eastern Iowa during the afternoon and evening hours. Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of this front. A few of these storms became severe. Hail up to the size of quarters fell with these storms as well as wind gusts over 60 MPH. A 65 MPH gust occurred as a thunderstorm moved across Walcott.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a tree down in Mt Vernon from thunderstorm winds. Report was from a picture on social media. Time of the event was estimated using radar.

A few of these storms became severe. Hail up to the size of quarters fell with these storms as well as wind gusts over 60 MPH. A 65 MPH gust occurred as a thunderstorm moved across Walcott.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service employee reported an estimated wind gust of 65 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved across northeast Iowa during the late morning and early afternoon of June 26th. These storms dropped some hail near Lime Springs (Howard County) that ranged in size from a nickel up to a quarter. These storms also produced wind damage just north of Lime Springs where trees and power lines were blown down and the doors to a metal machine shed were blown onto the roof of the shed. A private weather station measured a 67 mph wind gust in Waucoma (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 65 mph wind gust occurred in Lime Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed near a frontal zone pushing eastward across South Dakota and Nebraska. With sufficient elevated instability and deep-layer shear, a local enhancement in the larger-scale bow echo developed and produced a small area of damaging winds in extreme southeast South Dakota and adjacent northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A six-inch diameter limb was broken off a tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved across portions of northeast Iowa during the afternoon of July 11th. These storms produced some damaging winds that blew down trees in Riceville (Mitchell County) and in and near Elkader (Clayton County). Locally heavy rains from the storms caused some street flooding to occur in Waukon (Allamakee County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed and a shed blown over in Riceville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong line of storms developed near south central South Dakota and tracked southeast producing wind damage with gusts over 60 mph in Harrison and Monona counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported tress blown down with winds estimated between 50 to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong line of storms developed near south central South Dakota and tracked southeast producing wind damage with gusts over 60 mph in Harrison and Monona counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CO-OP observer reported estimated winds near 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed on the nose of the elevated instability gradient in the late night and early morning hours. With up to 1000 J/kg MUCAPE, a few storms produced large hail from northeast Nebraska into northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds brought down a 20 foot tall oak tree across a county highway. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front and remnants of a nocturnal mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) slowly moved across Iowa in the late morning to early afternoon hours. With moderate CAPE in the 1500-3000 J/kg range and shear of 20-30 kts, conditions were sufficient to support severe storm development. Storms produced winds gusting up to 70 mph at times, leaving behind significant tree damage in a few locations across north central and east central Iowa, along with periods of heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS measured a 53 knot wind gust at the Mason City airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large longwave trough was in place across much of the western and central US with an embedded shortwave. This provided a focus for storm development as it moved into a humid and unstable environment. Scattered convection eventually increased in coverage through the evening, producing quarter sized hail and damaging wind gusts. As convection grew upscale and moved into southern Iowa the severe threat diminished, though heavy rain continued to fall through the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported several trees blown down in the Camp Dodge area along 70th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms moved through the area on Tuesday, May 21. The bulk of the severe weather occurred during the late afternoon and evening time frame, with several Severe Thunderstorm Warnings issued over eastern Iowa into northwestern Illinois.|Straight-line winds were the main hazard with the storms and impressive wind gusts were observed. The strongest gust reported was 100 mph, which was from a personal weather station located just southeast of Atkins, IA! There were also several other locations that reported wind gusts in excess of 70 mph, and damage consistent with that.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limb snapped off, damaging a deck on a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a line of storms producing damaging winds and tornadoes passed across the area in the morning, a second wave passed over the area. The wave lacked strong forcing and present weak instability, but deep layer shear was high. At the same time, moisture fueling storm development did not make it closer to the surface and low levels were dry. This allowed for rapid evaporative cooling as precipitation fell, producing gusty winds with what appeared as rather weak storms on radar.

Scattered mixed-mode thunderstorms developed along a cold front which moved through an environment of 2000-2500 J/kg MLCAPE and up to 40 knots of deep-layer shear. A few brief supercells developed before evolving to bowing structures and brought marginally large hail and severe wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gust was measured at personal weather station KIAHAWAR11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) swept across Iowa late on May 6th and into the early morning hours of May 7th. The line was strongest in southern Iowa where the environment was more supportive of severe weather. Damaging winds and a few embedded tornadoes occurred as the line swept across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Personal weather station measured a 61 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet surging into the region, paired with increasing warm air and moisture advection into the morning hours of May 24th, led to the development of a line of showers and storms (QLCS) in Nebraska, which then swept across Iowa. MUCAPE values around 2000J/kg ahead of this line allowed for these storms to become severe within the QLCS as it rapidly tracked eastward across Iowa through the morning. Severe hail occurred with these storms, with values generally between 1-1.75 inches, though hail up to 2.5-2.75 inches was reported in Wayne county. Outside of the hail, severe winds were the primary hazard, with values reported generally between 58-75 mph. Widespread damage was reported due to the winds, with the majority of this damage being snapped trees/branches and power poles, some falling onto structures, as well as blown over semis. Outside of the hail and winds, a handful of tornadoes also occurred (please refer to the separate entry for those details).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report of estimated 70 mph winds and large branches down. Time of occurrence was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Troughing over the western CONUS brought southwesterly flow aloft over the central and northern Plains for the duration of this event. In the couple of days leading up to May 21st, the upper-level flow pattern amplified as a trough deepened over the northern Rockies. This trough then ejected out into the central Plains on the 21st. At the surface, a stationary front developed and stretched across central Nebraska and southern Iowa by the evening of the 19th. Elevated convection that morning north of the developing surface boundary resulted in isolated instances of severe hail and wind across southeast Nebraska. Additional scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed along this frontal boundary as it slowly lifted north, with a 64 miles per hour wind gust measured in Lancaster County and golf ball sized hail falling in Clarinda Iowa.|A weak low-pressure system developed the morning of the 20th in response to a shortwave rounding the base of the main trough out west, resulting in a cold front that pushed southeast across South Dakota and central Nebraska that afternoon. This front, however, stalled out across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa that evening, remaining draped across the region that night and into the morning of the 21st. Numerous rounds of strong and severe thunderstorms that night brought widespread severe weather and flash flooding, primarily across east-central Nebraska and southwest Iowa, though some elevated supercells in northeast Nebraska brought small hail to the area. After 10 UTC, an intense line of severe thunderstorms moved across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa bringing wind gusts ranging from 60 to 95 miles per hour, in addition to several spin-up tornadoes.  |As the primary upper level disturbance ejected out over the Great Plains, intense surface cyclogenesis was noted over eastern Nebraska. This resulted in the frontal boundary that had been draped across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa that night prior to be forced northward. By 18 UTC, a strong cold front pushed through southeast Nebraska and into Iowa that afternoon. Scattered supercell thunderstorms developed along this cold front and moved across southeast Nebraska and western Iowa. These supercells became tornadic after crossing the Missouri River into Iowa, with several long-track significant tornadoes being confirmed across the state. In the OAX CWA, the strongest tornado damage confirmed was that consistent of an EF-2 in Montgomery County. This tornado continued into the DMX CWA where it caused EF-3 damage. Another tornado developed in Page County shortly before 3 PM that afternoon. This tornado went on to produce EF-4 damage in the DMX CWA. All in all, over this three-day event, 10 tornadoes were confirmed across the OAX CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The office received a delayed report from the public of tree damage in Clarinda. A maple tree 18 inches in diameter was snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong negatively tilted trough lifted northward through the Western Plains during the afternoon and evening of May 6. While instability was not intense with around 1000 J/kg MLCAPE, a very strong low-level jet produced a considerable amount of boundary parallel deep-layer shear to entice a quick linear upscale growth, but the southwest-oriented shear in the 0-3km layer also led to brief mesovortices with enhanced wind corridors in segments which became oriented more northwest to southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gust was measured by Iowa mesonet station RSYI4 near Sibley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave passed across Iowa, providing focus for late afternoon and evening thunderstorms. The main instability axis remained south of the area, however modest instability was still available for storm growth. Steep midlevel lapse rates were available to support hail growth and dry low levels with DCAPE near 1000 J/kg supported strong wind gusts. Storms were strongest across central Iowa, procuring quarter sized hail and a few damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported branches down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rapidly developed over northern Iowa during the afternoon and organized into a powerful line of wind producing storms that surged through the Quad Cities and into northern and central Illinois through the evening hours.  These storms brought widespread damaging winds up to 100 mph for many locations, extending across eastern Iowa, much of Illinois, and into Indiana.  The Storm Prediction Center has classified this event as a Derecho which by definition is a swath of wind damage at least 400 miles long and at least 60 miles wide including several well-separated significant wind gusts over 75 mph.  Corridors of significant wind damage occurred along Highway 20. 10 tornadoes also occurred with these storms in the NWS Quad Cities service area.  ||In addition to the wind, very heavy rainfall fell in a short amount of time with many locations seeing over an inch. Flash flooding was reported in Cedar Rapids as well as in parts of Dubuque County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated thunderstorm wind speeds of 60 to 70 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, moist, and unstable air mass across the region ahead of a southward sinking cold front resulted in thunderstorm development, with several being severe. These produced large hail and scattered damaging winds. Most hail reports ranged in size from quarter to golf ball size hail. The largest hail fell in Van Buren County near Keosauqua where 2 to 3 inch diameter hail fell. There were also a few reports of 60 to 80 MPH across far southeast Iowa into west central Illinois, which damaged trees and knocked down several outbuildings.  The most widespread damage occurred near Oakville, IA.  Two brief, non-damaging tornadoes were also seen north of Macomb, IL.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report was relayed by broadcast media. 12-inch tree broken off about a foot above the base. Open farm outbuilding destroyed.  Pictures shared and show damage in one direction. Time of the event was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours on June 4th, an individual severe storm followed by a linear complex of storms produced sporadic wind damage across portions of northeast Iowa before moving into southeast Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station measured a peak wind gust from the south-southwest at 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of the 15th, upper air analysis showed a shortwave trough centered over the High Plains. At the surface, a warm front draped across the Plains slowly lifted north throughout the day, eventually stalling out across eastern Nebraska and down into northwestern Missouri. That afternoon, two areas of thunderstorm development was noted, one in northeast Kansas and another in northeast Nebraska. The thunderstorms in northeast Kansas quickly grew upscale into an MCS bringing strong winds and a brief spin-up tornado to southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa. The thunderstorms in northeast Nebraska were tied to a lingering MCV, with the storm mode initially remaining as clustered supercells. This brought large hail and several tornadoes across northeast Nebraska before finally growing upscale into a second MCS and tracking east into west-central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The office received a public report of tree limbs blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive cluster of thunderstorms developed across south central South Dakota in response to a weak mid-level wave drifting out of western South Dakota. A slight increase in the low-level jet brought deep-layer shear to 35-40 knots, and MUCAPE around 2000 J/kg provided fuel for storms to strengthen and congeal into a bowing structure which traversed to the east-southeast. The strongest storms produced damaging winds generally from 60 to 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds down several large tree branches in Merrill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a morning mesoscale convective system (MCS) across Nebraska, storms refired during the evening hours across northwest Iowa within fairly weak deep-layer shear and up to 2000 J/kg MUCAPE. Storms produced locally damaging wind as cores collapsed, and training of storms resulted in locally heavy rainfall of 2 to 3 inches around the Iowa Great Lakes in the matter of an hour or two.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds sporadically downed multiple power poles and trees in Correctionville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms erupted late evening on June 16 along a slightly elevated boundary with 2500-3500 J/kg MUCAPE and 30-35 knots of deep-layer effective shear. Initial storms quickly became widespread along the boundary from south central to east central South Dakota and evolved into a large bow echo, which moved through southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa with numerous reports of damaging winds mainly along and south of Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two cars were damaged at one residence and at least two other homes have trees and branches down on the buildings with minor damage due to thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and far northeast Missouri sat in the warm sector of an intense Spring storm system that brought widespread severe thunderstorms to the area during the afternoon and evening hours on April 16th. There were reports of hail, damaging winds, and two tornadoes. One of the tornadoes was on the ground for 42 miles across parts of Lee, Henry, Des Moines and Louisa Counties and was rated EF-2 with maximum winds of 130 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of two trees uprooted and several smaller trees snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-air analysis at 12 UTC on the 19th showed troughing over the eastern CONUS and ridging over the western CONUS. In the northern Great Plains, a shortwave trough embedded in northwesterly flow aloft dove southeast over the course of the day. At the surface, a strong low-pressure system was analyzed in the Dakotas with a warm front extending into the lower-Missouri River Valley, and a cold front extending west into the northern High Plains. This storm system dove southeast into the upper-Mississippi River Valley as the cold front surged south across the OAX forecast area. Very strong flow of 70 knots at the top of the boundary layer, aided by subsidence immediately behind the cold front brought very strong winds gusting 60 to 65 miles per hour during the early and mid afternoon hours. As a result of these strong winds, isolated power outages, downed trees, and overturned semis were all reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hours of high winds were observed during the afternoon hours. The strongest observed wind gust in the county was 59 miles per hour, observed by a personal weather station at 1:36 pm CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening hours on December 4th, an arctic cold front progressed through the region bringing strong winds along with it. Frequent wind gusts of 40-50 mph in Clayton County were responsible for tipping over a semi-trailer near St. Olaf.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 35 to 40 mph persisted across portions of Chickasaw County during the late morning through early evening hours on December 4th. Additionally, a 58 mph peak non-thunderstorm wind gust was observed at an Iowa Department of Transportation weather observation station in New Hampton during this period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind a strong cold front, deep-layer mixing created daytime winds which were sustained from 30 to 40 mph, with gusts as high as 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were sustained from 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 58 mph at Sheldon and 46 mph near Sutherland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front across Kansas and Missouri lifted north into Iowa the evening of September 5th, fueled by the increasing low-level jet. Shortly after midnight on the 6th, the front was situated over northern Iowa and convection out of Minnesota moved southeast into Iowa along the front and into a favorable convective environment as MUCAPE values rose between 2000 to 3000 J/kg. Steep mid level lapse rates of 8 C/km and deep layer shear of 50+ knots allowed for convection to quickly reach severe criteria. Golf ball sized hail and significant wind damage occured with these overnight storms. They were also efficient rain producers, with locations reporting between two to over 4 inches of rain in the heaviest cores.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station reported an 83 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushing across central South Dakota and Nebraska ignited severe storms overnight. With elevated instability of 1000-1500 J/kg and an increasing low-level jet, numerous storms formed along the 700 hPa baroclinic zone with a few taking on bowing characteristics.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Iowa RWIS station RSCI4, 1 mile southwest of Moville, measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms that developed in the early afternoon took advantage of the very high instability and had no trouble becoming strong, and even severe, in a short amount of time. Storms initially formed along and north of the Highway 30 corridor in eastern Iowa, produced widespread damage to crops in Buchanan and Delaware counties, and eventually went on to produce a wind gust near 60 mph at the Dubuque Regional Airport. Storms across this area eventually merged and slowed down, leading to localized rainfall amounts of 4-5 inches. This very heavy rainfall produced flash flooding in the Dubuque metro, with law enforcement reporting several road closures along Highway 20.|Storms continued to form along a line to the south through the mid afternoon, and reached the Mississippi River around 6-7 PM. Several reports of wind damage to trees were received from McDonough county to the Quad Cities metro area. A few reports of funnel clouds were also received from the Kirkwood, IL area, but no tornado was ever observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media shows a tree down and other large branches in Anamosa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois Saturday, July 11, producing widespread wind damage and very large hail. The first line of storms came through during the morning, producing damaging wind gusts and reports of quarter to ping pong ball size hail from Independence to Tipton Iowa. A wind gust of 65 MPH was measured just west of Independence. The second round of thunderstorms developed as a result of an upper level disturbance interacting with an atmosphere characterized by extremely high instability with modest vertical wind shear. Activity initially formed in north central Iowa, producing golf ball to two inch hail from Fort Dodge to Waterloo Iowa. Eventually, storms made their way towards the Cedar Rapids/Iowa City metro areas, producing widespread wind damage and hail up to baseball size. These storms continued through the Quad Cities metro east towards Princeton, Sterling, and Hennepin Illinois, producing additional reports of wind damage and winds of 70 to 90 mph. Numerous power outages were also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 6 inch diameter limb down at Brady Street and 29th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and quick moving cold front made its way through the Midwest. The environment ahead of the cold front was unseasonably warm, with temperatures in the upper 60s and readings in the 40s not far behind the front. This led to strong baroclinicity along the front, and subsequent convection with just the right amount of instability. This is considered a high shear low CAPE (HSLC) event, where organized severe thunderstorms are favored in this environment. Potential hazards are damaging winds, large hail, and tornadoes. Six tornadoes were confirmed and several wind reports were received (ranging between 50-60 mph, with a few gusts over 60 mph). Damage reports ranged from structural damage to tree damage. Many residents were left without power in parts of northwestern IL, leading to some schools canceling classes on the 11th. Besides the strong winds, a few severe hail reports were received.||In addition, heavy rain occurred with some of the stronger storms, with rainfall totals over an inch. For how dry it has been, flash flooding was not much of a concern. While the environment was very moist and favorable for heavy rainfall, the fast storm motions led to little accumulation through much of our forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter estimated a wind gust of around 60 mph with the thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front brought heavy rain and thunderstorms to eastern Iowa, northeast Missouri, and Illinois the evening of May 25th, 2020.  Flash flooding, and damaging winds were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported a tree blown down onto a home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level trough across the northern plains allowed for a region of broad ascent to support scattered convection across the area on the morning of June 26th. This activity remained sub-severe with the exception of a suspected microburst on the south side of the Des Moines metro which resulted in significant tree damage and power outages. As the trough pushed east a surface boundary moved across the state later in the day. While this also remained sub-severe, localized pockets of heavy rain did develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports of large tree limbs down across the area, some large enough to fold a chain link fence. Thousands were without power, which was restored by 830a. Radar data indicates likely microburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved across the midwest, pulling a surface boundary across the state. In the afternoon weak convection developed along this boundary, but most storms tended to be short-lived in nature with relatively weak shear profiles. These storms resulted in mainly wind damage reports across east-central Iowa. Convection continued to bubble through the evening with continued wind damage reports. Shortly after midnight convection out of the Dakotas made it to northern Iowa. This broken line of storms pushed across the state all night, with essentially no breaks in reports of large hail and damaging winds through sunrise. Additionally, some locations received heavy rain. Storms cleared the Des Moines metro area shortly after five in the morning and diminished in strength shortly after that.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Emergency Manager reported multiple large trees down with power outages. Time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures in the 90s and high moisture in the low levels led to diurnally driven thunderstorms on Friday, June 11th. Weak upper level support kept these storms relatively isolated and short-lived. Another focus area for convection was along a remnant outflow boundary from an MCS that morning, which led to some further storm development later in the day. One storm reached severe limits, which was seen in Burlington, Iowa. This storm resulted in strong downburst winds over 70 mph, that lead to multiple damaged trees and some buildings damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was partly down in Crapo Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front during the afternoon and evening of Wednesday, July 14.  Some of the storms produced damaging winds and numerous tornadoes.  Brief torrential rainfall was also observed with the stronger storm cells.  The areas that were hit hardest were in Buchanan, Delaware, Benton, Linn, and Jones counties in IA, where a total of 14 tornadoes occurred.  7 of the tornadoes had no visible damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cedar County Official estimated thunderstorm wind gusts up to 60 MPH generally 6 miles west of the Tipton area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement relayed a public report of 60 MPH thunderstorm winds near Springbrook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Iowa was sandwiched between a northern plains surface low and mid atlantic high pressure, firmly in a southwest flow regime with tight pressure gradient lending support|for gusty winds most of the day. Model soundings were exceedingly dry with 40 degree dewpoint depressions and winds at the top of the mixed layer pushing 50 knots at times. This resulted in elevated to extreme fire weather concerns across the state and a red flag warning was issued. Deep mixing sent many locations into record warm temperatures that afternoon and winds gusted over 40 mph. A couple locations in northern and central Iowa gusted over 55 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across a good portion of eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois on the afternoon of Tuesday August 24th, 2021. A corridor of very strong winds was observed along the Highway 20 corridor from Fairbank IA, to Hazelton IA and to Manchester IA, where numerous reports of damage to trees, homes and power outages were received. Hail up to golf ball size was also observed in Manchester IA.||Storms continued to track south and east, with an outflow boundary producing strong winds into portions of northwest Illinois including the Quad Cities metro. Additional reports of wind damage were received across northwest Illinois, with a 66 MPH wind gust measured at the Savanna, IL AWOS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system brought record warmth, high winds, and a serial derecho though Iowa and the Upper Midwest on December 15th.  This storm system produced a line of severe storms which continually produced damaging winds and tornadoes from Nebraska through much of Iowa. In eastern Iowa and Illinois, no tornadoes occurred, but widespread severe wind gusts occurred with brief heavy rain. ||Strong winds occurred through the day along with the record high temperatures. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across portions of eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois on the afternoon of Tuesday, August 10 2021. Storms produced mainly damaging winds in an environment with plentiful instability, with a gust estimated at 70 MPH occurring in Linn County. Damage to trees was reported in and around the Cedar Rapids metro east to Freeport IL. Some reports of hail were received as well, but were mainly at quarter-sized or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated supercell developed across south central South Dakota late evening along an advancing cold frontal boundary. With appreciable DCAPE and deep-layer effective shear, upscale growth occurred rapidly with the development of widespread damaging winds, which spread out of eastern South Dakota into northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large construction shed was lifted and thrown into a nearby corn field. Property damage costs are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated supercell developed across south central South Dakota late evening along an advancing cold frontal boundary. With appreciable DCAPE and deep-layer effective shear, upscale growth occurred rapidly with the development of widespread damaging winds, which spread out of eastern South Dakota into northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The doors of a school bus garage building were blown in by thunderstorm winds, which resulted in failure of the most of the roof and an exterior wall. Property damage costs are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable warm sector with MUCAPE up to 2000-2500 J/kg developed during the afternoon between an advancing cold front across southeast South Dakota and a gangly warm front extending into southwest Minnesota. An approaching weak impulse spurred rapid development of multiple rounds of storms near the warm front, initially discrete, with upscale growth into short bowing segments. Secondary development occurred during the evening along the cold front, which evolved into a slow-moving linear system with hail, wind, and heavy rainfall with shear vectors increasingly parallel to the boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous 3 inch tree limbs were downed by thunderstorm winds, along with several power poles. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following behind the rare December severe weather outbreak, very strong winds buffeted northeast Iowa from the late evening of December 15th into the morning of the 16th. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common with a few gusts over 60 mph. The highest measured wind gust was 63 mph at a mesonet station near Ionia (Chickasaw County). The high winds caused numerous power outages to occur across the area. The Governor of Iowa declared a state of disaster emergency for Allamakee, Chickasaw, Fayette, Floyd, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common across Winneshiek County during the late evening of December 15th into the early morning of the 16th. A mesonet station near Frankville measured a 58 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Along an earlier outflow-reinforced warm front, thunderstorms developed during the later evening with an increase in the low-level jet, and congealed into a small convective cluster in an environment with up to 3000 J/kg elevated CAPE.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds produced damage to large tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm traveled southeast from southwest Minnesota into northwest Iowa along with a compact mesoscale convective vortex. Unstable lapse rates led to brief  tornadic development along the track.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few large limbs from 1 to 3 inches in diameter were broken in a tree line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms along an advancing cold front merged to produce linear structures in the presence of 500-1000 J/kg DCAPE.  Short bowing segments with shear vectors weakly perpendicular to the boundary resulted in brief mesovortices.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a 10 inch diameter tree limb, with additional damage to corn fields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms moving from southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa intensified briefly with the help from an increasing low-level jet, tapping elevated instability of up to 2000 J/kg. Linear organization led to a brief wind enhancement.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds ripped off portions of the roofs on two hog barns. Property damage costs are a rough estimate. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed across the area with strong winds developing behind it. Deep mixing allowed for widespread gusts of 40-50 mph with a few locations briefly gusting to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved from Kansas across Iowa initiating severe storms and tornadoes in Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported roof damage and a flipped trampoline.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms tracked across all of eastern Iowa during the late afternoon and evening of Saturday March 5, 2022. Thunderstorms initially developed in southwest Iowa, and moved across central Iowa during the afternoon hours before reaching, our area after 600 PM. Initially, storms produced large hail and tornadoes over central Iowa, before the threat evolved more into damaging winds and tornadoes as storms moved into eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois. Winds around 60-75 MPH were reported from trained spotters and first responders, with confirmed reports of damage in some locations. EF-1 tornadoes occurred near Davenport and Vinton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio estimated a thunderstorm wind gust of 64 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms tracked across all of eastern Iowa during the late afternoon and evening of Saturday March 5, 2022. Thunderstorms initially developed in southwest Iowa, and moved across central Iowa during the afternoon hours before reaching, our area after 600 PM. Initially, storms produced large hail and tornadoes over central Iowa, before the threat evolved more into damaging winds and tornadoes as storms moved into eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois. Winds around 60-75 MPH were reported from trained spotters and first responders, with confirmed reports of damage in some locations. EF-1 tornadoes occurred near Davenport and Vinton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured a 52 knot wind gust.

Showers and thunderstorms developed along a cold front in the early morning hours, with quarter sized hail and a few stronger winds gusts reported in central Iowa around sunrise. This pushed east of the area by mid morning and temperatures warmed. This allowed for deep mixing and non-thunderstorm associated wind gusts of 50 to over 60 mph occurred across the area through the afternoon. In the evening a secondary front passed across the state, with daytime heating allowing for more robust convection to develop. The bowing, linear storm system primarily produced a swath of wind gusts and damage, however a few smaller hail reports were made.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 62 mph wind gust reported by NWS employee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Initial storms developed within the warm sector of a surface low that tracked northwest of the area, followed by a strong cold front with associated dry line that produced a quasi-linear (QLCS) storm mode later in the evening. Several tornadoes occurred across western into northern Iowa.  Initially the tornadoes were associated with supercell storms, however later storms produced QLCS tornadoes as it raced east northeast across the state. In addition to the tornadoes, hail up to tennis ball size occurred with initial super cells. The later QLCS produced hail up to golf ball sized along with damaging wind gusts over 70 mph at times.||Additionally, total rainfall in excess of 2 to 4 inches fell in a swath across areas that dealt with the severe thunderstorm and tornado warnings earlier in the event. While no flash flooding was experienced, rainfall in the Winnebago River basin above Mason City resulted in minor river flooding on April 13th, exceeding minor flood stage in the morning and receding below flood stage that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reports trees down in Kanawha. Time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms moved across northeast Iowa during the evening of April 12th. These storms produced five tornadoes and damaging wind gusts as high as 72 mph in Nora Springs (Floyd County). EF1 tornadoes occurred near Brownville (Mitchell County) and south of Riceville in both Mitchell and Howard Counties with both tornadoes damaging farm buildings and trees. EF0 tornadoes touched down in northern Floyd County into southern Mitchell County west of Orchard, near Saratoga (Howard County), and close to Ridgeway (Winneshiek County) with damage limited to trees and a few farm buildings. The Governor of Iowa issued a disaster proclamation for Howard, Mitchell, and Winneshiek Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 72 mph gust was measured in Nora Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection across Nebraska and Kansas in the early afternoon moved into western Iowa on the evening of May 12th. While loss of daytime heating resulted in a weakening of the thunderstorms, dry air across Iowa allowed for gusty winds to continue with the decaying thunderstorms. This included some tree damage reported in parts of northern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Medium sized tree branch down in Graettinger. Report via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front across Nebraska and northern Iowa served as the focus for midday convection, with a  few hail reports occurring in far northern Iowa. The associated surface low then moved into Iowa late in the day, resulting in additional isolated thunderstorms. One storm in west central Iowa produced a severe gust of 63 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Denison AWOS measured 55 knot gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Iowa was placed within the warm sector of a strong surface low pressure system, and the tight pressure gradient and deep mixing allowed for gusty south winds. A handful of station even gusted over 50 kts with some damage reported. Meanwhile, a dryline initiated convection across parts of northern Iowa in the late afternoon and evening. These storm primarily produced strong winds, though quarter sized hail was reported by one observer. Later that night, the cold front brought through another round of non-severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cattle barns a complete loss. Delayed report, time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops, powerlines, and structures along its path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak thunderstorm wind gust of 64 mph was measured by personal weather station KIAROCKR18 6 miles east of Rock Rapids. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Morning and afternoon convection moved across parts of southern Minnesota along a warm front. Later in the afternoon, convection developed in South Dakota and moved into Northern Iowa and southern Minnesota as the upper level shortwave trough axis passed over. Remnant outflow boundaries from the morning convection helped to steer the convection, which strengthened into a well organized mesoscale Convective Vortex (MCV). While the primary influences were over Minnesota, there was wind damage reported in northern Iowa with the trailing end of the convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of multiple trees and branches broken off. Largest 1.5 ft in diameter.

A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska. With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds. With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota.||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours. These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 60 mph was measured by Automated Weather Observing System site KSLB 3 miles west-southwest of Lakeside.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm downed a tree in Red Oak. The AWOS recorded a peak wind gust of 55 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported via social media of a tree downed by a severe storm wind gust. The Red Oak AWOS reported a peak wind gust of 55 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought much needed rainfall to eastern Iowa the morning of November 4th through the 5th. This also brought two rounds of severe weather to eastern Iowa. The morning of November 4th, a few storms cells intensified quickly and produced sporadic reports of hail that was penny to ping pong ball sized. Heavy rain also fell with this storm system with 48 hour rainfall totals of 1 to 4 inches across eastern Iowa with the heaviest rains being reported along a Ottumwa to Dubuque line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public relayed a report of 64 MPH recorded on a Davis Weather Station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought much needed rainfall to eastern Iowa the morning of November 4th through the 5th. This also brought two rounds of severe weather to eastern Iowa. During the morning hours of the 5th, a cold front moved eastward across the area couple with a low level jet led to a rapidly strengthening line of thunderstorms across far eastern Iowa that produced strong winds. The strongest winds along this line occurred in Scott and Muscatine County where damaging winds were reported. Heavy rain also fell with this storm system with 48 hour rainfall totals of 1 to 4 inches across eastern Iowa with the heaviest rains being reported along a Ottumwa to Dubuque line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at the Muscatine Airport measured a peak thunderstorm wind gust of 51 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed across the area with strong winds developing behind it. Deep mixing allowed for widespread gusts of 40-50 mph with a few locations briefly gusting to 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Marshalltown ASOS reported a 58 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong non-thunderstorm winds across portions of northwest Iowa produced isolated damage during the early morning hours of April 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds were between 40 and 50 mph at the Automated Surface Observing System (ASOS) at Spencer (KSPW) with a peak wind gust of 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong non-thunderstorm winds across portions of northwest Iowa produced isolated damage during the early morning hours of April 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System (ASOS) at Sioux City (KSUX) measured a wind gust to 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong wind gradient around a large upper level circulation resulted in widespread winds of 30 to 40 mph and gusts from 45 to 65 mph east of the James River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds periodically reached 40 to 45 mph with a peak gust of 60 mph at Automated Surface Observing System site KSPW near Spencer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong system developed on the morning of March 31st and tracked across the state of Iowa. This system pulled unseasonably warm and moist air into the Midwest, which combined with a very favorable wind profile to create a volatile environment for severe thunderstorms.  Discrete supercell thunderstorms occurred initially, but quickly transitioned into a QLCS mode. This system produced a historic tornado outbreak for the area, with a record 29 tornadoes on the day, including 13 rated EF-2, 1 rated EF-3, and 1 rated EF-4. This was the first EF-4 tornado in the NWS Quad Cities service area since 1995. 11 injuries were reported, with no known fatalities. In addition to the numerous tornadoes, there were many large hail and damaging wind reports. Maximum straight line thunderstorm winds were estimated at 80 to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60 knot gust measured by the ASOS at the Cedar Rapids Airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms initiated west of the area over portions of Nebraska and Kansas in the overnight hours of the 28th into the 29th, eventually leading into and across southern Iowa during the morning hours of the 29th. These storms were situated within a fairly supportive environment, including a north south gradient of MUCAPE values in the 2000 to 4000+ J/kg range, 30 to 40+ kts of effective shear, and steering flow supportive of MCS maintenance. Eventually, linear storm mode evolved into an MCV. Resulting storms produced a number of strong wind and wind damage reports, including trees downed and measured winds to 67 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lamoni Municipal Airport ASOS recorded a thunderstorm wind gust of 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system slowly dropping SE form the eastern Dakotas and SW Minnesota dragged a cold front across the state during the evening and overnight hours of the 24th of June. Within the warm sector, MUCAPE values peaked in excess of 3000 J/kg during peak heating and scaled back a bit overnight but remained around or in excess of 2000 J/kg. Effective shear values were supportive for organized convection, residing in the 35 to around 50 kt range. Morning and lingering convective activity likely played a role in initiating some storms with remnant outflow boundaries around. As a result, a number of isolated or clustered organized storms were seen during the evening and overnight hours and produced a number of reports of large hail and damaging winds. The largest hail reported was 3 inches in both Palo Alto and Black Hawk counties. A couple of funnel clouds were reported as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported, via social media, multiple large branches down on the road 2 miles west of Marne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Thursday, June 29, a powerful severe wind event moved over the region. This event was known as a derecho given its long duration from north of Kansas City to southern Indiana and its consistent strong intensity. Almost all of the damage in the area surveyed locally was due to straight-line winds, at times easily peaking between 100-120 mph in far western Illinois. Those who experienced the event conveyed how the strongest winds persisted for 10 to 15 minutes, and in some locations, it lasted as long as 20 minutes, which is atypical for derecho events.||There were some swaths of enhanced damage. These were largely due to intense wind surges in the local area. One of these in Clark county, Mo was determined to be a short-lived EF1 tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS storm survey found damage consistent with 100 mph straight-line winds. Damage was to dozens of large trees, downed utility poles, and a nearly destroyed outbuilding. A roof was off a home, and a couple mobile homes were severely damaged on the north side of Keokuk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system moved across eastern Iowa into southwest Wisconsin, sweeping a strong cold front through the area during the afternoon hours of Thursday, April 20th. A line of thunderstorms developed quickly along the front, with some of them becoming severe and producing hail in the counties along Mississippi River in eastern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser reported that a barn was destroyed on 223rd Avenue and 300th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Thursday, June 29, a powerful severe wind event moved over the region. This event was known as a derecho given its long duration from north of Kansas City to southern Indiana and its consistent strong intensity. Almost all of the damage in the area surveyed locally was due to straight-line winds, at times easily peaking between 100-120 mph in far western Illinois. Those who experienced the event conveyed how the strongest winds persisted for 10 to 15 minutes, and in some locations, it lasted as long as 20 minutes, which is atypical for derecho events.||There were some swaths of enhanced damage. These were largely due to intense wind surges in the local area. One of these in Clark county, Mo was determined to be a short-lived EF1 tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Storm Survey found damage to dozens of large trees and utility poles consistent|with peak wind gusts of 90 to 100 mph throughout the town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms tracked across southeast Iowa during the mid-afternoon into early evening of June 24, producing large hail up to golf ball size in Van Buren County and tennis ball size hail near Ottumwa.||More organized clusters of severe storms tracked through Dubuque into far northwest IL during the evening and late night hours of June 24.  Another storm cluster moved through the Quad Cities, producing wind gusts to 70 mph at the Davenport ASOS and damage to trees and power poles.  An EF-0 tornado also tracked east along and north of I-80 near the Davenport Airport, causing sporadic minor tree and structure damage.  This storm impacted the Quad City Airshow grounds, while no visitors were present.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down across roads, also a baseball diamond fence was damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were two rounds of severe thunderstorms on April 4. Round 1 of activity began during the early morning hours of April 4, 2023, as elevated thunderstorms developed across southeast Iowa. These storms quickly intensified and became severe as they moved into eastern Iowa, and produced very large hail from golf ball to tea cup size (2 inches). The largest hailstones were measured in and around the Davenport metro, where tea cup size hail was measured at the North Park Mall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported trees down on the road. The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Linear storms developed near a frontal boundary, moving across southeast South Dakota and into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota early on June 24. Within an environment of shear, a strong low-level jet, and strong DCAPE, some storms produced severe-level winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts downed up to 10 inch diameter tree limbs and damaged power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arc of convection developed ahead of a slow-moving upper-level low across central Nebraska. With around 2000 J/kg MUCAPE in a narrow zone and around 40 knots of deep layer shear, a few rotating severe storms developed with enhanced wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several medium-sized limbs were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a cold front in a deeply shear-parallel environment with 1000-2000 J/kg MLCAPE and quickly evolved into linear structures with spotty damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a result of thunderstorm wind gusts, a tree fell onto a parked car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a cold front in a deeply shear-parallel environment with 1000-2000 J/kg MLCAPE and quickly evolved into linear structures with spotty damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds gusts toppled a tree in Sergeant Bluff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An enhanced risk resulted in a numerous hail event across southwest Iowa. Storms developed after 4 PM along a stationary front near the Nebraska/Kansas border and moved into Iowa. A total of 55 storm reports were sent out by the office, most of which were for hail. The largest hailstone reported was 3 inches near Thurman. In addition to the hail, there were 3 observed tornadoes. Two in Fremont County and one in Pottawatomie County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported an estimated wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of storms impacted parts of northeast Iowa during the afternoon and into the evening of June 24th. Besides heavy rain, with 1.67 inches recorded in New Hampton (Chickasaw County) and 2.10 inches recorded near Elkport (Clayton County), damaging winds and hail also affected the area. Reports included tree damage in Oelwein (Fayette County) and 1 inch hail near Westgate (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 1 tree down and 1 large branch down in Oelwein.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms moved through the area on Tuesday, May 21. The strongest gust reported was 100 mph, which was from a personal weather station located just southeast of Atkins, IA! There were also several other locations that reported wind gusts in excess of 70 mph, and damage consistent with that.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter estimated wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lasting complex of severe thunderstorms, known as a derecho, tracked from central Nebraska through northwest Illinois during the late night hours May 23rd, to the morning of May 24th. This resulted in frequent wind damage reports and embedded tornadoes. There were 16 tornadoes confirmed in the NWS Quad Cities warning area. The four strongest tornadoes all had maximum winds near 110 mph (EF1), and those were the Delta, IA; Wellman to Frytown, IA; Bennett to Lowden, IA; and Coyne Center, IL tornadoes. This was the most tornadoes in a single event for the NWS Quad Cities warning area since the March 31, 2023 outbreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated around 70 mph, with tree limbs down around 3 to 4 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Nocturnal elevated thunderstorms developed due a strong low-level jet and steep mid-level lapse rates, with some spotty large hail in far southeast South Dakota. Farther north and east, storms weakened but developed a large stratiform region of rain, under which the strong synoptic flow was allowed to accelerate with areas of damaging wind gusts well away from storm updrafts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wake low winds were periodically sustained around 40 mph and gusted as high as 64 mph at Iowa Road Weather Information System site RSYI4 near Sibley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted upper level wave lifted across the Central Plains on April 16th, with timing of the dry slot ahead of the feature during the afternoon hours. With peak destabilization and development of storms near the triple point in the early to mid afternoon, a mix of hail, damaging winds, and several brief tornadoes occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow winds destroyed two grain bins.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient and elevated winds atop a well mixed boundary layer ahead of a low pressure system across Nebraska resulted in high winds across northwest Iowa mainly during the afternoon and evening hours of April 6th. Multiple automated stations reported maximum wind gusts at or above 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds briefly peaked near 40 mph during the afternoon hours. A maximum wind gust of 59 mph was observed at the Spencer Municipal Airport (KSPW).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: PLEASE NOTE, for tornadoes, please see separate Storm Data entry for May 21st. ||Multiple rounds of thunderstorms brought torrential rainfall, damaging winds, hail and destructive tornadoes to the state. These thunderstorms were the result of a low pressure system which lifted north into the area on the night of May 20th, 2024, producing slow moving storms that dropped 2 to 4 inches of rainfall. This resulted in flash flooding over portions of northern and central Iowa through the morning of May 21st, 2024. State level damage estimates to public infrastructure exceeded 7 million dollars due to the morning flooding.||Following the heavy rainfall through the morning, skies cleared out ahead of an approaching cold front. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rapidly developed over northern Iowa during the afternoon and organized into a powerful line of wind producing storms that surged through the Quad Cities and into northern and central Illinois through the evening hours.  These storms brought widespread damaging winds up to 100 mph for many locations, extending across eastern Iowa, much of Illinois, and into Indiana.  The Storm Prediction Center has classified this event as a Derecho which by definition is a swath of wind damage at least 400 miles long and at least 60 miles wide including several well-separated significant wind gusts over 75 mph.  Corridors of significant wind damage occurred along Highway 20. 10 tornadoes also occurred with these storms in the NWS Quad Cities service area.  ||In addition to the wind, very heavy rainfall fell in a short amount of time with many locations seeing over an inch. Flash flooding was reported in Cedar Rapids as well as in parts of Dubuque County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a large tree limb down and powerlines down. The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A derecho swept across South Dakota and into Iowa on the evening of July 5th. This resulted in a swath of  wind gust from 50 to 65 plus mph resulting in a number of power outages, tree damage, and a few damaged structures. A brief tornado occurred near Estherville with minor damage. As the bowing thunderstorm complex reach central Iowa the gusts had dropped below severe criteria given the less favorable environment with decreased deep layer shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees 12 inches in diameter down and power lines down. A few trees uprooted. Time estimated from radar.

A shortwave pushed across the Midwest, resulting a a period of severe storms. the leading moisture axis and warm front were confined main to Minnesota in the afternoon, though a few stronger storms skimmed northern Iowa with a hail report in Winnebago county. The cold front passed across Iowa later in the evening from western to northern Iowa. Modest CAPE was in place but with strong deep layer shear over 40 kts and drier low levels noted in model soundings, gusty winds were the primary hazard with this second round of thunderstorms. Winds gusted over 60 mph with a few reports of damage to trees and outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power poles down eat of Odebolt with damage to trees, a grain bin, and a shed. Delayed report, time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong system developed on the morning of March 31st and tracked across the state of Iowa. This system pulled unseasonably warm and moist air into the Midwest, which combined with a very favorable wind profile to create a volatile environment for severe thunderstorms.  Discrete supercell thunderstorms occurred initially, but quickly transitioned into a QLCS mode. This system produced a historic tornado outbreak for the area, with a record 29 tornadoes on the day, including 13 rated EF-2, 1 rated EF-3, and 1 rated EF-4. This was the first EF-4 tornado in the NWS Quad Cities service area since 1995. 11 injuries were reported, with no known fatalities. In addition to the numerous tornadoes, there were many large hail and damaging wind reports. Maximum straight line thunderstorm winds were estimated at 80 to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported power lines and trees down at 32nd Street and Peru Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon thunderstorms developed in the exit region of a strong jet streak in northwest flow aloft, focused along a prefrontal trough as it moved from southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa. Storms quickly became severe, with MLCAPE of up to 1500 J/kg and deep layer shear around 40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple three to four inch diameter tree limbs were downed by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection began in early afternoon in eastern Iowa along a subtle boundary across the state. This was fueled by high CAPE exceeding 3000 J/kg with deep layer shear sufficient to support large hail growth. Later in the evening a secondary round of convection moved across the area producing additional hail in the form of a cold pool driven MCS, driven along the plume of higher instability. The result was dozen of hail reports up to baseball sized across much of central and southern Iowa, damaging homes and vehicles and taking down power lines. As storms crossed Interstate 80 an accident resulted in significant delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trailers blown over at a dealership south of Grinnell. Report via social media.

A mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) moved out of Nebraska and South Dakota, into Iowa in the afternoon of June 17th. Increased forcing with the MCV met with increasing instability as CAPE reach 1500-2000 J/kg in the afternoon. Storms developed quickly in a line, with a few isolated cells remaining on the southern end of the line. While deep shear was only around 25 kts, this was enough to sustain updraft capable of producing severe hail. Several locations in southwest Iowa reported quarter sized hail with one instance of thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees down along Raccoon River Valley Trail between Redfield and Linden. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot and humid air in place across eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and far northeast Missouri interacted with a southward moving frontal boundary and disturbance aloft, setting the stage for development and organization of severe thunderstorms across the region. Storms initially formed from north central Iowa and southern Wisconsin along the frontal boundary, which slowly worked south into the hot, humid air in place across the region. As storms arrived across east central Iowa, they began producing winds around 70 MPH across the Hwy 20 corridor. Another storm to the south further intensified, and began producing winds of 80+ MPH as it tracked along the Hwy 30 corridor. This impacted the northern suburbs of Cedar Rapids into Clinton. These storms continued to produce wind gusts around 60-70 MPH as they progressed into northwest Illinois. One brief EF-0 tornado began in Clinton, Iowa, then moved across the Mississippi River before dissipating near Fulton, Illinois. Damage near Andrew, Iowa in Jackson County was determined to be straight-line winds. Storms additionally produced large hail around the Dubuque metro, with quarter size hail reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down along Highway 62, near Andrew.

Storms initially formed from north central Iowa and southern Wisconsin along the frontal boundary, which slowly worked south into the hot, humid air in place across the region. As storms arrived across east central Iowa, they began producing winds around 70 MPH across the Hwy 20 corridor. Another storm to the south further intensified, and began producing winds of 80+ MPH as it tracked along the Hwy 30 corridor. This impacted the northern suburbs of Cedar Rapids into Clinton. These storms continued to produce wind gusts around 60-70 MPH as they progressed into northwest Illinois. One brief EF-0 tornado began in Clinton, Iowa, then moved across the Mississippi River before dissipating near Fulton, Illinois. Damage near Andrew, Iowa in Jackson County was determined to be straight-line winds. Storms additionally produced large hail around the Dubuque metro, with quarter size hail reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS at the Dubuque Airport reported a 58 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the latter half of the afternoon along a slow moving cold front.  Dry air poised off the surface allowed for outflow dominant storms to develop, with a brief period of damaging gusty wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several larger limbs were broken off trees by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rapidly developed over northern Iowa during the afternoon and organized into a powerful line of wind producing storms that surged through the Quad Cities and into northern and central Illinois through the evening hours.  These storms brought widespread damaging winds up to 100 mph for many locations, extending across eastern Iowa, much of Illinois, and into Indiana.  The Storm Prediction Center has classified this event as a Derecho which by definition is a swath of wind damage at least 400 miles long and at least 60 miles wide including several well-separated significant wind gusts over 75 mph.  Corridors of significant wind damage occurred along Highway 20. 10 tornadoes also occurred with these storms in the NWS Quad Cities service area.  ||In addition to the wind, very heavy rainfall fell in a short amount of time with many locations seeing over an inch. Flash flooding was reported in Cedar Rapids as well as in parts of Dubuque County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager reported that multiple powerlines were with all of Brady Street blocked across from Theisens. The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection from a passing upper level trough and associated MCS in the morning brought an initial round of non-severe storms mainly in Minnesota into the far reaches of northern Iowa. However, severe weather potential increased by the afternoon as the aforementioned trough and surface boundary made its way into Iowa. Conditions ahead of the boundary included 40-50 knot values of 0-6 km bulk shear, along with 3000-4000J/kg of instability, 25-30 knots of 0-3 km bulk shear and DCAPE values around 1000-1500 J/kg. As a result, a line of showers and storms developed in the afternoon and passed over central Iowa into the evening, with a handful of hail reports in central Iowa generally from penny-size to 2 inches, while the greater majority of reports involved severe wind gusts of 55-70+ mph and associated damage. Tornadic activity within the line of storms did occur, with a tornado developing in the Des Moines Metro area, causing primarily significant tree damage, as well as some structural and power-pole damage in the area as well. Heavy rain given plenty of warm and moist air over the area also resulted in isolated flash flooding, specifically over Black Hawk county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust report from a personal weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized line of thunderstorms developed across western South Dakota during the evening hours of July 13 and spread eastward through the night. The environment featured 2000-3000 J/kg MUCAPE, 25 to 35 knots of deep-layer shear, and an increasingly warm and dry capped layer further eastward through South Dakota. Widespread damaging wind developed with the mesoscale convective system with gusts from 60 to 75 mph common. The storms weakened rapidly as they entered northwest Iowa, but still managed to cause some damage just over the border from South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several small trees and numerous branches in Beloit.

These storms brought widespread damaging winds up to 100 mph for many locations, extending across eastern Iowa, much of Illinois, and into Indiana.  The Storm Prediction Center has classified this event as a Derecho which by definition is a swath of wind damage at least 400 miles long and at least 60 miles wide including several well-separated significant wind gusts over 75 mph.  Corridors of significant wind damage occurred along Highway 20. 10 tornadoes also occurred with these storms in the NWS Quad Cities service area.  ||In addition to the wind, very heavy rainfall fell in a short amount of time with many locations seeing over an inch. Flash flooding was reported in Cedar Rapids as well as in parts of Dubuque County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of an 8 inch tree limb down blocking the road. The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms lined up across central Iowa on the 850 mb moisture gradient early in the morning of July 31, producing up to quarter sized hail and gusts over 50 mph in isolated downbursts. Storms shifted into southeast Iowa through the morning and remained strong, producing hail and strong winds. As storms shifted southeast the orientation shifted to allow for training storms will heavy rain of 3-6 inches in some locations. This area also received heavier rain the previous day when multiple rounds of storms moved across the area, so soils were less able to handle the additional rain. Areas of flash flooding resulting in road closures were reported. After a break in the afternoon and early evening, a bowing line of storms passed across central to southern Iowa. This brough another round of strong winds of 55 to 65 mph and isolated hail up to quarter sized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS station KAIO with gusts over 50 mph for at least 10 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms lined up across central Iowa on the 850 mb moisture gradient early in the morning of July 31, producing up to quarter sized hail and gusts over 50 mph in isolated downbursts. Storms shifted into southeast Iowa through the morning and remained strong, producing hail and strong winds. As storms shifted southeast the orientation shifted to allow for training storms will heavy rain of 3-6 inches in some locations. This area also received heavier rain the previous day when multiple rounds of storms moved across the area, so soils were less able to handle the additional rain. Areas of flash flooding resulting in road closures were reported. After a break in the afternoon and early evening, a bowing line of storms passed across central to southern Iowa. This brough another round of strong winds of 55 to 65 mph and isolated hail up to quarter sized.

Scattered convection occurred throughout the day across the area as a weak cold front dropped slowly south across the area. By afternoon the front had largely stalled near the I-80 corridor and more vigorous convection developed along the front, fed by high instability exceeding 3000 J/kg and steep mid level lapse rates of 7 to 8 C/km. Storms first began near Newton and then back built towards the west along the boundary through the evening. Storms produced large hail and stretching resulted in a huge number of funnel clouds, including pictures with a least visible at the same time. Later in the evening a wave across Nebraska moved into Iowa, sweeping east along the instability gradient that the earlier evening convection developed on. A few damaging wind gusts occurred with this line of storms, however it remained sub severe most of the night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large 10 inch diameter branch broken from a healthy walnut tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 22nd, an initial round of thunderstorms produced localized flash flooding in Fayette County. A secondary round of storms moved through northeast Iowa during the afternoon, producing localized flooding and wind damage in Allamakee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management reported trees down near Dorchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and far northeast Missouri sat in the warm sector of an intense Spring storm system that brought widespread severe thunderstorms to the area during the afternoon and evening hours on April 16th. There were reports of hail, damaging winds, and two tornadoes. One of the tornadoes was on the ground for 42 miles across parts of Lee, Henry, Des Moines and Louisa Counties and was rated EF-2 with maximum winds of 130 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of an estimated 58 MPH wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of showers and thunderstorms during the day on April 27th as a pair of disturbances lifted northeastward across the area ahead of a cold front. These storms produced hail and damaging wind gusts as they moved across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Dispatch relayed a report of a large tree down on a vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a morning mesoscale convective system (MCS) across Nebraska, storms refired during the evening hours across northwest Iowa within fairly weak deep-layer shear and up to 2000 J/kg MUCAPE. Storms produced locally damaging wind as cores collapsed, and training of storms resulted in locally heavy rainfall of 2 to 3 inches around the Iowa Great Lakes in the matter of an hour or two.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power poles leaning on the west edge of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed late in the day on Aug 27th in northeast Missouri and spread into southeast Iowa and far western Illinois into the evening. Large hail up to golf ball size fell for nearly one half hour straight in Rutledge, MO.||A pronounced downburst (powerful winds that descend from a thunderstorm and spread out quickly once they hit the ground) caused widespread damage in Burlington, IA, knocking down trees, limbs, and powerlines. Five homes had holes in the roofs due to falling limbs. A commercial storage building had a partial roof collapse and another large tall outbuilding had partial roofing peeled back, consistent with wind estimates of at least 80 mph. ||Rainfall totals of 2 to 4 inches were observed in parts of northeast Missouri and southeast Iowa. ||Oppressive heat and humidity that began on the 26th and persisted into the 27th helped spawn these storms. Both Dubuque (tied) and Moline (exceeded) daily record highs on the 27th, with Moline reaching 98�� and Dubuque 94��.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five houses had holes in roofs from fallen tree branches. A storage facility had a partial  roof collapsed, and a roof was peeled back on a tall outbuilding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-air analysis at 12 UTC on the 19th showed troughing over the eastern CONUS and ridging over the western CONUS. In the northern Great Plains, a shortwave trough embedded in northwesterly flow aloft dove southeast over the course of the day. At the surface, a strong low-pressure system was analyzed in the Dakotas with a warm front extending into the lower-Missouri River Valley, and a cold front extending west into the northern High Plains. This storm system dove southeast into the upper-Mississippi River Valley as the cold front surged south across the OAX forecast area. Very strong flow of 70 knots at the top of the boundary layer, aided by subsidence immediately behind the cold front brought very strong winds gusting 60 to 65 miles per hour during the early and mid afternoon hours. As a result of these strong winds, isolated power outages, downed trees, and overturned semis were all reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Though no direct measurements were made in the county of severe level wind gusts, observations from surrounding counties support the high likelihood of wind gusts up to 58 mph occurring the afternoon of the 19th.
