EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a cold front in the early morning hours, with quarter sized hail and a few stronger winds gusts reported in central Iowa around sunrise. This pushed east of the area by mid morning and temperatures warmed. This allowed for deep mixing and non-thunderstorm associated wind gusts of 50 to over 60 mph occurred across the area through the afternoon. In the evening a secondary front passed across the state, with daytime heating allowing for more robust convection to develop. The bowing, linear storm system primarily produced a swath of wind gusts and damage, however a few smaller hail reports were made.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several farm buildings damaged along highway 169. Delayed report, time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the later morning and early afternoon on May 30th across southeast South Dakota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs, but strong deep-layer forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mobile mesonet station measured a 62 mph wind gust. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Initial storms developed within the warm sector of a surface low that tracked northwest of the area, followed by a strong cold front with associated dry line that produced a quasi-linear (QLCS) storm mode later in the evening. Several tornadoes occurred across western into northern Iowa.  Initially the tornadoes were associated with supercell storms, however later storms produced QLCS tornadoes as it raced east northeast across the state. In addition to the tornadoes, hail up to tennis ball size occurred with initial super cells. The later QLCS produced hail up to golf ball sized along with damaging wind gusts over 70 mph at times.||Additionally, total rainfall in excess of 2 to 4 inches fell in a swath across areas that dealt with the severe thunderstorm and tornado warnings earlier in the event. While no flash flooding was experienced, rainfall in the Winnebago River basin above Mason City resulted in minor river flooding on April 13th, exceeding minor flood stage in the morning and receding below flood stage that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Personal weather station recorded a 60 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the approach of a strong mid-level wave, a cluster of multicellular storms evolved within a weakly unstable and weakly-sheared environment.  A fair amount of dry air at lower levels made for 600 J/kg or more of DCAPE, with storms producing some spotty significant wind damage along a track from near Paullina to around Primghar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A farm outbuilding was destroyed by thunderstorm winds. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th. With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward. Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 60 mph was measured by the Automated Surface Observing System site KSPW 4 miles west-northwest of Spencer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed with a strengthening low-level jet during the overnight hours. With MUCAPE of around 1000 J/kg and 30 knots of effective shear, isolated storms produced up to quarter size hail and damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple tree branches from 4 to 6 inches in diameter were downed by thunderstorm winds on the Northwestern College campus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front plowed into an environment with roughly 1500 J/kg MLCAPE but weak shear, which resulted in numerous thunderstorms with brief intense updrafts. The strongest storms produced isolated damaging wind gusts and hail up to golf ball size. By evening, a stronger low-level flow ahead of the front resulted in a slightly stronger storm with hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter across northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 61 mph was measured by a personal weather station 5 miles northwest of Washta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms moved across portions of northeast Iowa during the morning of June 15th. These storms produced some wind damage with trees and power lines blown down near Rudd (Floyd County). A farm in rural northwest Winneshiek County sustained considerable damage with downed trees and damage to the house and outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down near Rudd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms moved across portions of northeast Iowa during the morning of June 15th. These storms produced some wind damage with trees and power lines blown down near Rudd (Floyd County). A farm in rural northwest Winneshiek County sustained considerable damage with downed trees and damage to the house and outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down at a farm in rural northwest Winneshiek County. One of the trees fell onto the porch of the farm house. Metal was blown off two metal outbuildings and a livestock trailer was rolled down a hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of severe thunderstorms moved through Iowa and Illinois the evening of June 21st. These produced heavy rain totals and areas of damaging winds, especially in Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of severe thunderstorms moved through Iowa and Illinois the evening of June 21st. These produced heavy rain totals and areas of damaging winds, especially in Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust at the small grass strip airport 7 miles west of Bellevue Iowa. Public report was relayed through broadcast media.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a public report of trees blown down and damage to power poles in Charlotte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest wind increased to 25 to 35 mph, with numerous gusts to 45 to 55 mph across northwest Iowa following an overnight frontal passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 59 mph was measured by the automated surface observing system site KSPW 3 miles west northwest of Spencer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread significant damaging wind storm tracked across nearly all of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa on the morning of July 12. These storms produced widespread winds of 60 to 80 mph, with embedded stronger downbursts with wind speeds up to 100 mph. The strongest measured wind gust was 82 mph in Dodge County NE, but some of the high-end straight line wind damage supports embedded wind speeds up to 100 mph.||This storm also produced a few embedded tornadoes. Some of the tornado tracks were only identified by narrow swaths of damage through mature cornfields, with minimal damage otherwise and peak wind speeds similar to the nearby downburst winds. All but one of the tornadoes was preceded by a Wireless Emergency Alert. There were 0 reported injuries or fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported trees blown down at the golf course.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough across the Rockies underwent lee cyclogenesis, pulling a warm front north into Iowa in the afternoon. While a few storms developed along the warm front, most development was along the later cold front that moved across the area in the evening. These storms were frequent hail producers, with many reports of baseball sized hail or larger. A tornado also occurred which resulted in damage to a few homes, out buildings, and trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shed blown over, time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system tracked across the state of Iowa on Friday, March 31st. This system pulled unseasonably warm and moist conditions into the Midwest, which coupled with a very favorable wind profile and created quite the volatile environment for severe thunderstorms. This produced several long lived supercells which developed across central Iowa during the afternoon.  The storms were prolific hail producers along with a few reports of wind damage as they moved rapidly northeast.  Eventually, the storms produced a few tornadoes before exiting into eastern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Non thunderstorm wind gust associated with boundary behind the line of storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot and humid air in place across eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and far northeast Missouri interacted with a southward moving frontal boundary and disturbance aloft, setting the stage for development and organization of severe thunderstorms across the region. Storms initially formed from north central Iowa and southern Wisconsin along the frontal boundary, which slowly worked south into the hot, humid air in place across the region. As storms arrived across east central Iowa, they began producing winds around 70 MPH across the Hwy 20 corridor. Another storm to the south further intensified, and began producing winds of 80+ MPH as it tracked along the Hwy 30 corridor. This impacted the northern suburbs of Cedar Rapids into Clinton. These storms continued to produce wind gusts around 60-70 MPH as they progressed into northwest Illinois. One brief EF-0 tornado began in Clinton, Iowa, then moved across the Mississippi River before dissipating near Fulton, Illinois. Damage near Andrew, Iowa in Jackson County was determined to be straight-line winds. Storms additionally produced large hail around the Dubuque metro, with quarter size hail reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported a 65 mph wind gust with the thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching upper level jet within the presence of a sharp lower- to mid-level boundary focused widespread convective development during the overnight hours. Elevated instability of 1000-1500 J/kg and deep layer shear in excess of 40 kts produced a very organized bowing system, which resulted in widespread 60 to 80 mph wind reports in southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa. At the peak, several thousand people were without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds were measured on a personal weather station in 4 miles north-northeast of Sergeant Bluff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of convection moved across Iowa in quick succession beginning in the pre-dawn hours of July 12th. This first was an area of convection that developed along an elevated boundary into northwest Iowa. While this area of storms mainly remained sub-severe, severe hail did fall across central Iowa near 6 am. At the same time a MCS moved out of the Dakotas and into western Iowa, moving quickly and eventually merging with the first area of storms. This second round developed into a line of storms with winds gusting up to 70 mph, fueled by the low level jet. Orientation of 0-3 km shear was favorable for quick spin-ups and two weaker tornadoes did form in Cass county that morning. With deep warm cloud layers over 4000 meters and precipitable water nearing 2, areas that received rain in both rounds received two to four inches by the time storms ended late in the morning. An isolated pocket of four to six inches fell in Greene and Boone counties, creating flash flooding in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report, peak gust of 65 mph at personal weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front sank south across Iowa during the afternoon and evening of July 28th. The environment was primed for severe storms with warm moist air in place; as temperatures climbed into the 90s and dewpoints into the 70s. By afternoon MLCAPE values of 3000 J/kg were in place across much of Iowa with deep layer shear exceeding 40 kts. Such an environment supported rapid storm development along the boundary by late afternoon. Storms produced ping-pong ball to baseball sized hail along with two tornadoes, damaging confinement pens and creating easily identifiable tracks on high resolution satellite imagery through surrounding fields. As storms pushed south into the evening, the hazards transitioned to a wind threat. A micro burst occurred in Marshall County very near the ASOS, resulting in an isolated pocket of 80 to 90 mph winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured by Marshalltown ASOS.

At the same time a MCS moved out of the Dakotas and into western Iowa, moving quickly and eventually merging with the first area of storms. This second round developed into a line of storms with winds gusting up to 70 mph, fueled by the low level jet. Orientation of 0-3 km shear was favorable for quick spin-ups and two weaker tornadoes did form in Cass county that morning. With deep warm cloud layers over 4000 meters and precipitable water nearing 2, areas that received rain in both rounds received two to four inches by the time storms ended late in the morning. An isolated pocket of four to six inches fell in Greene and Boone counties, creating flash flooding in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of 70 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary from earlier day convection focused a gradient of instability and storm development with additional destabilization from heating by early afternoon. Boundary interactions from sluggish storms led to training of heavy rainfall cores, as well as a couple brief tornadoes. Extensive flooding resulted in a state disaster declaration for Sioux County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very old barn collapsed under the weight of a more modern roof. The structure fell to the southeast, most likely in relation to rear flank downdraft wind on the backside of tornado producing mesocyclone. Property damage costs are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of severe thunderstorms impacted the area on Sunday May 7, 2023.  Two areas of storms were present during the mid to late afternoon, one entering the Cedar Rapids and Iowa City metros and another around the Burlington and Fort Madison metros. The first was the most severe, producing hail up to baseball size in Koszta, Iowa and widespread wind damage, especially around West Liberty, Iowa. This storm, and storms to the west, eventually merged into a cluster of severe thunderstorms, producing large hail and destructive winds around 70-75 mph all the way to Mount Pleasant, Iowa and Macomb, Illinois. The latter produced sporadic reports of quarter to half dollar sized hail, along with a brief landspout tornado east of Yorktown, IL.  There was also severe blowing dust which was especially bad near I-55 in north central Illinois. Even more thunderstorms developed overnight across the area, and mainly produced torrential rain and isolated large hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree branches down in town, with multiple smaller limbs. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe thunderstorms developed across much of eastern Iowa on the evening of May 6, 2023. Storms mainly produced hail up to golf ball size. Through the evening, storms merged and weakened in intensity, but not before producing some wind damage across northern and central Lee County. An isolated tornado also occurred in Muscatine County. Heavy rain also fell from storms as some remained stationary for long periods of time. Most notably, locations north and east of Fairfield, Iowa recorded over two inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inch diameter tree limbs downed by strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the end of August, ridging was noted across the eastern two-thirds of the CONUS. This upper-level pattern and associated surface high pressure system brought afternoon highs in the upper 90s and low 100s in addition to dew points in the 70s and lower 80s to much of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. As a result, excessive heat was experienced by many the afternoons of both the 25th and the 26th, where heat indices ranged from 110 to 125 degrees during peak heating in the afternoon. |By the 26th, a shortwave trough ejected into the Pacific Northwest and moved across the northern Rockies. Isolated supercells developed the afternoon of the 26th along a weakening cold front that was slowly pushing south into northern Nebraska. While most of the severe weather from these storms remained north and west of the Omaha NWS County Warning Area, one supercell did make its way into portions of northeast Nebraska bringing hail up to 3.25 inches in diameter.|By the 29th, another shortwave trough ejected into the northern Plains immediately behind the disturbance that brought large hail to portions of northern Nebraska on the 26th. A surface low-pressure associated with this disturbance developed in south-central Canada, with a secondary low pressure that developed near the North Dakota-South Dakota border. A surface cold front was also noted as extending from northeast South Dakota into eastern Colorado. Scattered thunderstorms developed along and ahead of this front across much of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the early afternoon hours. While most of these thunderstorms remained under severe limits, there were a few instances of wind gusts ranging from 60 to 70 miles per hour, and one report of quarter sized hail from northeast Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported an old oak tree that had been snapped about 20 feet up the trunk. Several smaller branches were also noted as being blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another robust shortwave passed across the area. The warm front draped across the area provided the focus for thunderstorm development in the afternoon. Initial more discrete convection developed with and environment supportive of large hail with sufficient instability and large looping hodographs favoring rotating updrafts. The storms grew upscale into the early evening, producing a line of storms with damaging wind gusts across southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power pole and lines down on Highway 5 a mile and a half north of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure brought multiple rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms to portions of central Iowa on Tuesday, April 16th, 2024. Elevated thunderstorms developed overnight and lingered into the morning across the area, remaining sub severe. This was followed by a second line of storms that lifted into the area by late morning. This line produced a few tornadoes in southwest to central Iowa. This was then followed by a third round of thunderstorms, more discrete in storm mode, that produced a few more tornadoes in northwest portions of Iowa. At least 8 short-lived tornadoes touched down, some causing damage in portions of central Iowa from late morning through the afternoon. A few of the severe thunderstorms also produced hail of 1 to 2 inches in diameter and wind gusts of 50-60 mph. Environmental winds behind the dry line were quite strong as well, occasionally gusting in excess of 50-55 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Livestock building sustained damage during structural repair. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over Wyoming moved east through the morning and deepened rapidly, creating quickly intensifying southeasterly winds in the afternoon across the area. Winds gusted over 40 mph across the area and were strongest in the west with gusts over 50 mph. A few locations in western Iowa reported damage associated with the winds. The wind was also accompanied by very dry air so a Red Flag Warning was in effect at the time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A grocery store facade was damaged, sufficient enough to close the store.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient and elevated winds atop a well mixed boundary layer ahead of a low pressure system across Nebraska resulted in high winds across northwest Iowa mainly during the afternoon and evening hours of April 6th. Multiple automated stations reported maximum wind gusts at or above 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds briefly peaked between 40 and 43 mph during the afternoon hours. A maximum wind gust of 58 mph was observed at the Sheldon Regional Airport (KSHL). In addition, small tree limbs were reported broken in Sanborn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient and elevated winds atop a well mixed boundary layer ahead of a low pressure system across Nebraska resulted in high winds across northwest Iowa mainly during the afternoon and evening hours of April 6th. Multiple automated stations reported maximum wind gusts at or above 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds briefly peaked near 40 mph during the afternoon hours. A maximum wind gust of 58 mph was observed at Sioux Gateway Airport (KSUX). In addition, emergency management reported several trees and power lines down around Sioux City. And broadcast media reported sections of large trees up to two feet in diameter downed four miles southeast of Sioux City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted upper level wave lifted across the Central Plains on April 16th, with timing of the dry slot ahead of the feature during the afternoon hours. With peak destabilization and development of storms near the triple point in the early to mid afternoon, a mix of hail, damaging winds, and several brief tornadoes occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds destroyed a horse lean-to and injured the horse inside.  Several tree limbs were also broken.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew a trailer into the ditch along Highway 75 around one-half mile south of Merrill.  A photo showed the trailer was destroyed in the process.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of April 30th, upper-air analyses showed a jet max ejecting out into the central/northern Plains, trailing behind a short-wave trough. Divergence aloft at the apex and just downstream from the trough brought synoptic scale forcing for ascent across much of the area by early afternoon. At the surface, south-southwesterly winds aided in boundary layer moistening with the transport of dew points into the 60s across much of southeast Nebraska into southwest Iowa. A dry-line was also noted to extend from east-central Nebraska southwest into central Kansas. This set the stage for scattered thunderstorm development by early afternoon.|By late morning, scattered showers and thunderstorms were already underway across portions of western Iowa, slowly moving to the northeast. By 1:30 pm, towering cumulus eventually gave way to thunderstorm development along and just ahead of the dry line, with the first storms initiating along a line from southeastern Stanton County, southeast into western Cass County. By 3:30 pm, thunderstorms had been ongoing or beginning to form along the entire stretch of the Missouri River through the OAX CWA. A couple thunderstorms in southwest Iowa and southeast Nebraska by mid-afternoon exhibited strong rotation which warranted tornado warnings. However, no tornadoes were reported. The vast majority of severe reports from these storms in the CWA were large hail, some of which were significant in size (greater than 2 inches in diameter).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station recorded a wind gust of 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient and elevated winds atop a well mixed boundary layer ahead of a low pressure system across Nebraska resulted in high winds across northwest Iowa mainly during the afternoon and evening hours of April 6th. Multiple automated stations reported maximum wind gusts at or above 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds briefly peaked between 40 and 43 mph during the afternoon hours at Storm Lake Municipal Airport (KSLB).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet surging into the region, paired with increasing warm air and moisture advection into the morning hours of May 24th, led to the development of a line of showers and storms (QLCS) in Nebraska, which then swept across Iowa. MUCAPE values around 2000J/kg ahead of this line allowed for these storms to become severe within the QLCS as it rapidly tracked eastward across Iowa through the morning. Severe hail occurred with these storms, with values generally between 1-1.75 inches, though hail up to 2.5-2.75 inches was reported in Wayne county. Outside of the hail, severe winds were the primary hazard, with values reported generally between 58-75 mph. Widespread damage was reported due to the winds, with the majority of this damage being snapped trees/branches and power poles, some falling onto structures, as well as blown over semis. Outside of the hail and winds, a handful of tornadoes also occurred (please refer to the separate entry for those details).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines down as reported from emergency manager.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large scale bow echo developed across Nebraska in the late night hours and pushed northeast toward the lower Missouri River valley and northwest Iowa.  Winds associated with the convective line were as high as around 70 mph in the Sioux City metro area, and caused considerable tree damage and power outages to around 2000 customers. The roof on one home in the Morningside area of Sioux City was damaged by a falling tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple large tree limbs were broken off by thunderstorm outflow winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms moved across the area bringing hail and damaging wind gusts to central Iowa. The first round moved across the area around midday as remnants of an MCS out of Kansas and Nebraska moved into the area. By afternoon a front lifted across the area and afternoon heating allowed for thunderstorm redevelopment, becoming severe with MLCAPE exceeding 2000 J/kg and increasing deep layer shear. Later in the evening a line of thunderstorms moved out of Nebraska and into Iowa, sustained by a strong low-level jet. This bowing line of storms was mostly sub severe, but did result in thunderstorm wind damage late in eastern portions of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter measured a wind speed of 59.3 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave arrived into Iowa from the Dakotas on the afternoon of May 27th, with around 1000 J/kg of MUCAPE over the area. Scattered showers and storms developed mainly over northern and eastern Iowa, which resulted in a few reports of severe hail of penny to quarter size, as well as a report of a severe wind gust around 60mph mainly east of I-35. Wind damage was reported in Marshall county, with no other reports of damage otherwise. Due to the presence of surface vorticity with some enhanced stretching over northern Iowa, a funnel cloud also occurred in the afternoon, particularly over Kossuth county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report of several trees down in town. In addition, up to 2 inches in depth of half to three-quarter inch diameter hail. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following morning convection over central and southern Iowa tied to a system further south into Missouri, a frontal boundary sweeping across northern into the eastern parts of Iowa by the afternoon to evening resulted in additional showers and thunderstorms. The parameter space overhead included MLCAPE values of 1000+ J/kg and deep layer shear around 40-50+ knots. In particular, Cerro Gordo county received severe thunderstorm wind damage, with estimated gusts around 58 mph. Otherwise, no other reports were received for this particular event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report of a boat dock, tree, and garage door damage around Clear Lake. Time of occurrence estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing warm and moist air into Iowa, along with a shortwave arriving into the afternoon to evening and developing LLJ led to the development of areas of showers and thunderstorms. Favorable shear and instability parameters overhead led to at least a few severe thunderstorms, generally occurring over northern into parts of central Iowa. In particular, Humboldt county down to Story county experienced severe hail ranging between 1-1.5 inches, largest in the town of Bode, as well as thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power pole down just north of Bode on 120th street. Time of occurrence estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing warm air advection supported scattered convection, however the lack of surface boundary and modest parameter space prevented storms from becoming organized. A few storms developed across far southern Iowa in the warm air advection wing in the early evening. While these largely remained sub-severe,  a single storm did produce a 59 mph gust at the Lamoni ASOS. Based on the radar signature, the environment with increasing dry air aloft, and isolated nature of the report, it is suspected that this was a microburst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lamoni ASOS reported a gust of 59 mph. Likely a microburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following showers and thunderstorms over Iowa during the morning hours with a passing shortwave, an additional shortwave arriving in the Central Plains with the aid of increased lift and MUCAPE values around 1000 J/kg overhead brought another round of scattered showers and storms into the afternoon and evening hours. These multiple rounds of convection, along with the slow-moving nature of some storms led to heavy rainfall and eventually flash flooding, particularly over Wright county. Reports of flooded roads and rainfall totals averaging between 3-5 inches were common. Severe wind was also reported in Adair county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Personal weather station report of 64mph thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The prevailing upper-level pattern for the end of July featured a trough over the Pacific Northwest, favoring southwesterly flow over the central Great Plains. Two troughs ejected out into the north-central CONUS during this episode. The first brought two back to back nights of severe weather across portions of northeast Nebraska the night of the 28th and the 29th. The second trough moved out into the central Plains and brought the synoptic scale forcing for a historic severe wind event that impacted both Lincoln and Omaha metropolitan areas. Wind gusts as high as 100 miles per hour extended from Lancaster County northeast to the Nebraska-Iowa border, leaving behind a large swath of significant tree, powerline and structural damage. Power outages from this storm lasted for up to a week in some locations, particularly in Omaha. ||This episode also featured several afternoons of excessive heat for portions of southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa. A few instances of 110 degree heat indices were observed in a few southwest Iowa counties the afternoon of the 29th, with even more widespread excessive heat the afternoon of the 31st across much of the forecast area south of Interstate 80. Peak heat indices in this region topped out between 110 and 115 degrees before afternoon convection cooled temperatures back down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Department of Highways station measured a 61 miles per hour wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A PV anomaly entering the region and associated surface boundary interactions, with increasing forcing overhead and favorable deep layer shear, resulted in scattered on and off showers and thunderstorms throughout much of the day. This resulted in a handful of severe wind reports and isolated wind damage, particularly in Clarke and Marshall counties with gusts generally ranging between 60 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured wind gust of 62mph, with 1 to 2 inch limbs broken off trees. Nearly an inch of rain in 30 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of severe storms impacted Iowa on July 30th. This first round occurred through the early morning as a bowing line of storms moved into the area from Nebraska around 1 am CDT. This passed across the area through the early morning hours, exiting into southeastern Iowa by 5 am. Several sites reported wind gusts of 60-65 mph as this line of storms swept across the area. A second bowing segment passed across the are in the mid to late morning, this time bringing hail along with the winds. In its wake, thunderstorms developed on the outflow boundary across central to southeast Iowa. These storms were slow moving and produced heavy rain along with a number of quarter sized and larger hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of tree damage in tow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Mesoscale Convective Vortex (MCV) moved through the region on the morning of June 16. This quick moving system produced widespread winds between 60 to 70 mph through it's path, especially for areas along and east of the Mississippi River. Isolated reports of hail were noted, with the largest being golf ball sized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 12 inch tree branch was downed by the thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unorganized line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed in eastern Iowa on the afternoon of June 3. This line of thunderstorms continued into northwest Illinois, with thunderstorms capable of severe winds and heavy rain. A quick 1-3 inches of rainfall fell for some, which resulted in localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured from a personal weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moving into the region resulted in showers and thunderstorms into the previous evening and continuing into the early morning hours on June 16th over the western half of Iowa. MLCAPE values around 1000-2000 J/kg and 20-30 knots of bulk shear allowed for a favorable environment to produce a few storms that become severe, with thunderstorm wind damage reported in Sac and Webster counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees down in town and the power is out. Possible structure damage in town also.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous slow-moving storms occurred on the afternoon of June 25, lasting into the evening. These storms quickly became strong to severe, with multiple becoming supercells, which ended up producing locally heavy rainfall, damaging winds, large hail, and four tornadoes that were generally weak, but still capable of damage. Winds were generally 60 mph or greater with the stronger storms, with a corridor of winds greater than 65 mph. These thunderstorms remained nearly stationary in areas, resulting in nearly 2 to 4 inches of quick rainfall and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter estimated thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours on June 4th, an individual severe storm followed by a linear complex of storms produced sporadic wind damage across portions of northeast Iowa before moving into southeast Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down 1 mile north of Bassett.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous slow-moving storms occurred on the afternoon of June 25, lasting into the evening. These storms quickly became strong to severe, with multiple becoming supercells, which ended up producing locally heavy rainfall, damaging winds, large hail, and four tornadoes that were generally weak, but still capable of damage. Winds were generally 60 mph or greater with the stronger storms, with a corridor of winds greater than 65 mph. These thunderstorms remained nearly stationary in areas, resulting in nearly 2 to 4 inches of quick rainfall and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five large trees and many branches downed at Saulsbury Bridge Recreation Area. One tree took down a powerline. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms moved through the area on Tuesday, May 21. The bulk of the severe weather occurred during the late afternoon and evening time frame, with several Severe Thunderstorm Warnings issued over eastern Iowa into northwestern Illinois.|Straight-line winds were the main hazard with the storms and impressive wind gusts were observed. The strongest gust reported was 100 mph, which was from a personal weather station located just southeast of Atkins, IA! There were also several other locations that reported wind gusts in excess of 70 mph, and damage consistent with that.

During the evening hours on August 5th, a line of severe thunderstorms brought strong winds to portions of northeast Iowa. Additionally, an embedded supercell thunderstorm would go on to produce three tornadoes in northeast Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported both a tree onto a roadway and loss of power in Bluffton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon and evening hours on August 29th, storms of various modes moved across portions of northeast Iowa bringing strong winds causing several instances of tree and structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a 4-inch diameter tree limb down three miles west of Colwell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper air analysis depicted a negatively tilted trough over the western CONUS, with its axis extending from the central Rocky Mountains northwest into the Pacific Northwest. At the surface, a low-pressure center was deepening over the northern High Plains along with a secondary surface low in western Nebraska. Extending south out of these pressure centers was a Pacific cold front. At 21 UTC, a warm front was noted to extend southeast from southwest Nebraska into northeast Oklahoma. As the evening progressed, strong warm air advection ahead of the mature low-pressure pushed the warm front north into eastern Nebraska by 00 UTC. ||Along this warm front and ahead of the Pacific cold front, several lines of strong to severe thunderstorms moved through eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. The first line of strong storms moved through central and northeast Nebraska after 4:30 PM CDT. No severe reports were associated with these storms in the OAX CWA. Another intensifying segment of the same line moved into southeast Nebraska by 7:30 PM CDT, this time south of the northward advancing warm front. These storms tracked northeast through southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa, producing numerous severe wind gusts across the area and multiple spin-up tornadoes. The vast majority of wind gusts recorded with these storms ranged from 55 to 70 miles per hour. The strongest wind gust was measured at 71 miles per hour in Richardson County. 2 EF-0 tornadoes were confirmed in southeast Nebraska, both in Gage County. There were also an additional 3 tornadoes confirmed in southwest Iowa, all EF-1 tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 65 miles per hour wind gust was measured by a personal weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-air analysis showed a trough over the northern Rockies the evening of May 23rd. That night, this trough ejected out into the central and northern Great Plains. At the surface, a low-pressure developed in western South Dakota. Extending south and southwest of this surface low into Colorado was a cold front. A secondary low-pressure center was noted in northwestern Kansas where the surface cold front intersected a dry line that extended south into western Texas. |Shortly after midnight, a line of severe thunderstorms along this cold front moved into the OAX CWA. From 05 UTC through 10 UTC, this line of storms brought widespread severe wind gusts up to 110 miles per hour and 17 spin-up tornadoes across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. These storms continued to be severe and tornadic through the morning of the 24th, later being classified as a derecho. |Troughing over the western CONUS continued to bring southwesterly flow aloft the region even behind this first trough. By the evening of the 25th, another shortwave trough had amplified over the Great Basin and had shifted over the Rocky Mountains. Ahead of this disturbance, a low-pressure developed in northwest Kansas, with a warm front extending east-southeast across northern and eastern Kansas. The evening of the 25th, scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed across this frontal zone in northern Kansas and generally tracked to the east-northeast. As such, scattered thunderstorms moved into southeast Nebraska after 9 PM CDT. While largely remaining below severe limits, severe straight-line winds were observed along the south and east side of the Omaha metro as a thunderstorm tracked from western Otoe into eastern Pottawattamie County. It was estimated wind gusts reached as high as 70 miles per hour with this storm, with power outages being reported due to the winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 69 miles per hour wind gust was measured by a personal weather station.

From 05 UTC through 10 UTC, this line of storms brought widespread severe wind gusts up to 110 miles per hour and 17 spin-up tornadoes across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. These storms continued to be severe and tornadic through the morning of the 24th, later being classified as a derecho. |Troughing over the western CONUS continued to bring southwesterly flow aloft the region even behind this first trough. By the evening of the 25th, another shortwave trough had amplified over the Great Basin and had shifted over the Rocky Mountains. Ahead of this disturbance, a low-pressure developed in northwest Kansas, with a warm front extending east-southeast across northern and eastern Kansas. The evening of the 25th, scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed across this frontal zone in northern Kansas and generally tracked to the east-northeast. As such, scattered thunderstorms moved into southeast Nebraska after 9 PM CDT. While largely remaining below severe limits, severe straight-line winds were observed along the south and east side of the Omaha metro as a thunderstorm tracked from western Otoe into eastern Pottawattamie County. It was estimated wind gusts reached as high as 70 miles per hour with this storm, with power outages being reported due to the winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 62 miles per hour wind gust was recorded on a personal weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) swept across Iowa late on May 6th and into the early morning hours of May 7th. The line was strongest in southern Iowa where the environment was more supportive of severe weather. Damaging winds and a few embedded tornadoes occurred as the line swept across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Personal weather station recorded 65 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: PLEASE NOTE, for tornadoes, please see separate Storm Data entry for May 21st. ||Multiple rounds of thunderstorms brought torrential rainfall, damaging winds, hail and destructive tornadoes to the state. These thunderstorms were the result of a low pressure system which lifted north into the area on the night of May 20th, 2024, producing slow moving storms that dropped 2 to 4 inches of rainfall. This resulted in flash flooding over portions of northern and central Iowa through the morning of May 21st, 2024. State level damage estimates to public infrastructure exceeded 7 million dollars due to the morning flooding.||Following the heavy rainfall through the morning, skies cleared out ahead of an approaching cold front. This line of storms also produced damaging winds, leading to widespread tree damage and power outages throughout the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported multiple large trees down, including some on buildings. This is a delayed report and time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet surging into the region, paired with increasing warm air and moisture advection into the morning hours of May 24th, led to the development of a line of showers and storms (QLCS) in Nebraska, which then swept across Iowa. MUCAPE values around 2000J/kg ahead of this line allowed for these storms to become severe within the QLCS as it rapidly tracked eastward across Iowa through the morning. Severe hail occurred with these storms, with values generally between 1-1.75 inches, though hail up to 2.5-2.75 inches was reported in Wayne county. Outside of the hail, severe winds were the primary hazard, with values reported generally between 58-75 mph. Widespread damage was reported due to the winds, with the majority of this damage being snapped trees/branches and power poles, some falling onto structures, as well as blown over semis. Outside of the hail and winds, a handful of tornadoes also occurred (please refer to the separate entry for those details).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Personal weather station recorded a wind gust of 69.5 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet surging into the region, paired with increasing warm air and moisture advection into the morning hours of May 24th, led to the development of a line of showers and storms (QLCS) in Nebraska, which then swept across Iowa. MUCAPE values around 2000J/kg ahead of this line allowed for these storms to become severe within the QLCS as it rapidly tracked eastward across Iowa through the morning. Severe hail occurred with these storms, with values generally between 1-1.75 inches, though hail up to 2.5-2.75 inches was reported in Wayne county. Outside of the hail, severe winds were the primary hazard, with values reported generally between 58-75 mph. Widespread damage was reported due to the winds, with the majority of this damage being snapped trees/branches and power poles, some falling onto structures, as well as blown over semis. Outside of the hail and winds, a handful of tornadoes also occurred (please refer to the separate entry for those details).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inch and a quarter sized hail, along with 1-2 foot diameter trees toppled on garages and homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave arrived into Iowa from the Dakotas on the afternoon of May 27th, with around 1000 J/kg of MUCAPE over the area. Scattered showers and storms developed mainly over northern and eastern Iowa, which resulted in a few reports of severe hail of penny to quarter size, as well as a report of a severe wind gust around 60mph mainly east of I-35. Wind damage was reported in Marshall county, with no other reports of damage otherwise. Due to the presence of surface vorticity with some enhanced stretching over northern Iowa, a funnel cloud also occurred in the afternoon, particularly over Kossuth county.

A Mesoscale Convective Vortex (MCV) moved through the region on the morning of June 16. This quick moving system produced widespread winds between 60 to 70 mph through it's path, especially for areas along and east of the Mississippi River. Isolated reports of hail were noted, with the largest being golf ball sized.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at Charles City reported a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwave troughs traversing the northern CONUS brought unsettled weather to the area for the end of June. On the 21st, a stationary front was draped across northeast Nebraska into Iowa. Ahead of one of the aforementioned shortwave troughs, several rounds of strong thunderstorms developed near this frontal zone and moved across northeast Nebraska. At 8 pm on the 21st, radar imagery showed training thunderstorms had set up across central Nebraska. These storms moved into east-central Nebraska dropping 2 to 6 inches of rain across the Platte River Basin. As a result, minor flooding was observed along the Platte River in Dodge, Saunders, Douglas and Sarpy Counties. Further north in Knox County, additional rounds of thunderstorms brought flooding to the Ponca Creek, prompting emergency management to deploy sand bags. Flooding in these locations continued for several days before subsiding the 23rd.||Behind this first shortwave, a shortwave ridge moved out into the central Plains accompanied by a surface high pressure system. Afternoon high temperatures in the 90s and dew points in the mid and upper 70s brought excessive heat to much of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa the afternoons of the 24th and the 25th. Excessive heat was most widespread on the 24th, with heat indices across the entire WFO ranging from 107 to 120 degrees. Afternoon thunderstorms on the 25th mitigated excessive heat, though heat indices did hit 110 in far southwest Iowa.||On the 25th, another shortwave trough brought more unsettled weather to the region. A surface front was draped across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa when the first convection initiated over downtown Omaha. This storm quickly became intense, producing multiple instances of 3 to 4 inch hail in the Omaha metro. As this storm weakened over eastern Sarpy County, additional strong to severe thunderstorms developed across northeast Nebraska. While the most widespread hazard was large hail up to the size of golf balls, two tornadoes were confirmed in Madison County. Later that evening, a bow echo developed and moved through the northern Omaha Metro, bringing wind gusts up to 90 miles per hour at Eppley Airfield and into northern Pottawattamie County. An additional line of storms developed after dark that night across southeast Nebraska and produced scattered damaging wind gusts, the highest of which was an 83 mph wind gust measured by emergency management in Jefferson County.||The unsettled weather at the end of June brought widespread excessive rain to much of southeast South Dakota and Northwest Iowa. Rainfall estimated from these storms showed a swath of 4 to 8 inches of rain that fell between Sioux Falls and Sioux City. The result of this rain and additional rain across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa was river flooding along the Missouri and Little Sioux the last week of June and into July. Flooding during this time frame resulted in numerous road closures, including I-29 between Council Bluffs and Missouri Valley. This stretch of the interstate was closed beginning midnight the night of the 25th, and remained closed through the end of the month. Levee breaches in Monona County on the 25th prompted several evacuations, as resultant flash flooding inundated multiple county roads and homesteads. No injuries or fatalities were reported from this. While flooding subsided in and north of the Omaha metro by the end of the month, river flooding along the Missouri persisted into the first week of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of the swath of significant damage winds from Douglas County. This storm continued to produce winds in excess of 90 miles per hour as it moved through western portions of Pottawattamie County. Just like in northeastern Douglas County, multiple roads were closed due to down trees and powerlines. Winds continued to weaken with eastward extent after the storm moved across Interstate 80, though severe wind gusts and damage were reported as far east as Oakland where numerous trees and branches were down across the town. Emergency management reported several houses with trees on them in addition to building debris being blown several blocks in some instances.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, moist, and unstable air mass across the region ahead of a southward sinking cold front resulted in thunderstorm development, with several being severe. These produced large hail and scattered damaging winds. Most hail reports ranged in size from quarter to golf ball size hail. The largest hail fell in Van Buren County near Keosauqua where 2 to 3 inch diameter hail fell. There were also a few reports of 60 to 80 MPH across far southeast Iowa into west central Illinois, which damaged trees and knocked down several outbuildings.  The most widespread damage occurred near Oakville, IA.  Two brief, non-damaging tornadoes were also seen north of Macomb, IL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive cluster of thunderstorms developed across south central South Dakota in response to a weak mid-level wave drifting out of western South Dakota. A slight increase in the low-level jet brought deep-layer shear to 35-40 knots, and MUCAPE around 2000 J/kg provided fuel for storms to strengthen and congeal into a bowing structure which traversed to the east-southeast. The strongest storms produced damaging winds generally from 60 to 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds overturned a semi truck in a parking lot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 12th, a low-amplitude shortwave embedded in northwesterly flow aloft ejected out into the central Great Plains. At the surface, a surface low-pressure was noted in North Dakota with a cold front extending south into South Dakota. Ahead of this cold front, a surface trough extended from south-central Nebraska into northeast Nebraska. Isolated thunderstorms developed on this surface trough late in the afternoon across far northeast Nebraska. These storms quickly intensified and dove south-southeast along the Missouri River. While a brief tornado was observed near Decatur, Nebraska, the more impactful hazard from these storms were the large hail they produced. Long swaths of golf ball to baseball sized hail fell along and just west of the Missouri River during the evening hours with these storms, including in the Omaha metro where baseball sized hail fell from downtown south into Bellevue.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a measured wind gust of 65 miles per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms erupted late evening on June 16 along a slightly elevated boundary with 2500-3500 J/kg MUCAPE and 30-35 knots of deep-layer effective shear. Initial storms quickly became widespread along the boundary from south central to east central South Dakota and evolved into a large bow echo, which moved through southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa with numerous reports of damaging winds mainly along and south of Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts to 59 mph were measured by a personal weather station in Lake Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On September 19th, a lone severe thunderstorm developed a very impressive hail core aloft as it moved into Howard County. As it progressed through Howard County, it dropped quarter sized hail southeast of Riceville which damaged crops. Later into the evening, additional storms developed near the Mississippi River bringing isolated wind damage to Clayton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported damage to a barn via social media east of Elkader. The barn had its door blown in and half of the roof blown off.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a corn field flattened along the east side of State Highway 52.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a morning mesoscale convective system (MCS) across Nebraska, storms refired during the evening hours across northwest Iowa within fairly weak deep-layer shear and up to 2000 J/kg MUCAPE. Storms produced locally damaging wind as cores collapsed, and training of storms resulted in locally heavy rainfall of 2 to 3 inches around the Iowa Great Lakes in the matter of an hour or two.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs from 10 to 12 inches in diameter fell onto power lines from the electric pole to garage.  Winds were accompanied by hail up to dime size and torrential rainfall. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon on September 21st, a supercell thunderstorm briefly passed through northeastern Allamakee County. As the storm moved through it produced small hail and some minor tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported tree branches down on State Highway 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed along a boundary draped across northern Iowa on the afternoon of September 19th with additional storms through the evening on a cold front that moved across the area later in the evening. With over 2000 J/kg of available MLCAPE, steep lapse rates, and modestly curved hodographs that supported rotating updrafts, hail was reported in several of the storms that developed. Storms later into the evening produced wind damage in parts of southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report. Two maple trees blown down. Damage to the top of an oak tree. Power lines damaged due to fallen trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed late in the day on Aug 27th in northeast Missouri and spread into southeast Iowa and far western Illinois into the evening. Large hail up to golf ball size fell for nearly one half hour straight in Rutledge, MO.||A pronounced downburst (powerful winds that descend from a thunderstorm and spread out quickly once they hit the ground) caused widespread damage in Burlington, IA, knocking down trees, limbs, and powerlines. Five homes had holes in the roofs due to falling limbs. A commercial storage building had a partial roof collapse and another large tall outbuilding had partial roofing peeled back, consistent with wind estimates of at least 80 mph. ||Rainfall totals of 2 to 4 inches were observed in parts of northeast Missouri and southeast Iowa. ||Oppressive heat and humidity that began on the 26th and persisted into the 27th helped spawn these storms. Both Dubuque (tied) and Moline (exceeded) daily record highs on the 27th, with Moline reaching 98�� and Dubuque 94��.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of widespread wind damage in the Burlington area. Limbs, powerlines, and trees down blocking residential streets, tree down on home. Time was estimated using radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the mid-afternoon into the early evening on December 4, a powerful cold front stretching from a weather system in the northern Great Lakes region swept through eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois. This front ushered in much colder air along with very strong gradient winds. None of the wind gusts were associated with any thunderstorms. Several wind gusts over 58 mph were recorded across the region, and several semi trailers were blown over in northeast Iowa near Dubuque.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported by Cedar Rapids Airport automated weather equipment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving into western Iowa by the evening hours, paired with a warm and moist environment ahead of it into Iowa resulted in the development of showers and storms. Specific environmental parameters that were favorable for storms to develop and grow upscale along the cold front included wind shear values around 30-35 knots, along with 1500-2000 J/kg of MLCAPE and 1000+ J/kg of DCAPE.  Though severe winds were the most probable hazard, reports received were almost none. One report of thunderstorm wind damage came from Humboldt county, where several large tree branches broke off trees in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large tree branches down at this location, with additional damage up to 2 miles north of this area.
